## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  Was mechanical ceremony repair; now carries a net-new Rust static-analysis fixture with a real design surface. Three mechanical tracks (ceremony retirement cleanup, claimed-done fixes, verification sweep) frame one design-laden track: a BCG command-compliance linter in theurge. The linter needs judgment; the rest is mechanical. The locked decisions below are settled — do not relitigate them; mount-time minting of the new module/fixture name is the open naming call.
  
  ## Locked decisions (2026-05-31 design conversation, Opus 4.8)
  
  - **Track 2 is a Rust theurge lint, not bash.** The external-command audit becomes a static-analysis fixture in theurge's **fast test suite** — not a bash step in `rbq_qualify_release()`. It rides `rbw-ts.fast` (dev loop) and, via the `complete` suite, `rbw-tr` (release gate), which the ceremony's Step 4 runs pre-strip. That coverage is what lets ceremony **Step 2 be deleted**.
  - **BCG is the single source of truth.** Encode the allowlists from BCG section "Command Dependency Discipline": the POSIX floor, the declared-dependency set (RBS0 inventory + `openssl`), and the **eviction table — enforced**, failing with BCG's prescribed replacement (e.g. `grep` -> `case`/`[[ =~ ]]`). This is stricter than today's Step 2, whose hand-rolled POSIX list wrongly permits evicted commands.
  - **Algorithm: two-pass, function-aware.** Collect every locally-defined function across the corpus; then flag each command-position token not in {bash builtins, local functions, POSIX allowlist, declared deps}, and fail-with-replacement on the eviction table. Soundness rests on presumed BCG-compliance (shellcheck already gates the corpus in `rbq_qualify_fast`) — a command-position scan suffices; no heredoc/case parser.
  - **Checks everything pre-veiling.** Lints ALL `Tools/` bash including `vov_veiled/`. The linter carries **no veil-scope knowledge** — it is a linter, not veiling logic, and must not couple to either packaging path (`vof` kit-parcel collection, or the ceremony strip). The only partition is by **execution environment**: kit-bash (strict BCG) vs GCB-bash (`rbgja/`, `rbgjb/`, `rbgjv/` — looser; gcloud et al. present). **Encode the GCB-domain allowlist now**; do not skip those dirs.
  - **First-run cleanup is in-pace.** Checking veiled bash for the first time may surface real eviction-table violations; fix them in this pace, do not split to a follow-on. Expect the first run not to land green.
  - **Track 4 resolved.** `OPEN_SOURCE_UPSTREAM` pointing at `recipebottle-staging.git` is **intended**. No remote mutation. Ensure no ceremony/doc text asserts the non-staging URL as canonical.
  
  ## Tracks
  
  ### Track 1 — Ceremony retirement cleanup
  
  In `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md`: Step 8 — drop the retired `cp README.consumer.md README.md` (keep the `CLAUDE.consumer.md` copy); the lowercase `readme.md` removal is a no-op (case-insensitive alias of the tracked `README.md`) — replace it with defensive `git rm -f --ignore-unmatch index.html .nojekyll`; Step 10 survival list — remove `.nojekyll` and `index.html` (both retired); staging — keep `git add CLAUDE.md` (Step 8 overwrites it), drop the explicit `README.md` add (`git add -u` covers it). Step 2's dependency-source line vanishes with Step 2 (Track 2).
  
  ### Track 2 — BCG command-lint fixture (locked above)
  
  Build per the locked decisions. Model on the host-side fast-fixture pattern (`rbtdrf_fast.rs`: `dir: &Path -> rbtdre_Verdict`, registered in `rbtdrm_manifest.rs`, added to the `fast` suite). Mint a fresh anchor word — `audit` is taken (image-domain cult verb). Then delete ceremony Step 2.
  
  ### Track 3 — Verify claimed-done
  
  Item B is a FIX: correct the phantom `tt/rbw-tf.QualifyFast.sh` -> `tt/rbw-tq.QualifyFast.sh` in ceremony Step 11 (`rbw-tf` is FixtureRun). Items C (Step 9 marshal-zero ordering before the Step 10 strip) and D (Step 3 regime-completeness check present) verify-and-close.
  
  ### Track 5 — Verification sweep
  
  After Tracks 1-4 land: retirement grep (no live `index.html`/`README.consumer.md`/`.nojekyll` outside retired gallops/memos); foundry colophon grep (no stale `rbw-h*` foundry refs in consumer-visible sources post b63dc6cf); fast qualify `tt/rbw-tq.QualifyFast.sh`; onboarding smoke (`tt/rbw-o.ONBOARDING.sh` + one role track); notch. No auto-wrap — operator decides the close.
  
  ## Verification gates
  
  Each track presented before executing. Review point before committing the ceremony diff. The lint fixture's first full run (including veiled bash) is a discovery step, not a pass gate — surface violations before fixing. No remote mutation. No auto-wrap.
  
  ## References
  
  - `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` — ceremony repair target
  - `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BCG-BashConsoleGuide.md` section "Command Dependency Discipline" — allowlist source of truth (POSIX floor, eviction table, declared-dep principle)
  - `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrf_fast.rs`, `rbtdrm_manifest.rs` — fast-fixture shape + suite/manifest registration
  - `Tools/rbk/rbq_Qualify.sh` — `rbq_qualify_fast`/`rbq_qualify_release`; shellcheck sibling; Step 2 deletion rationale
  - `Tools/vok/vof/` — existing kit-parcel veiling (`vofr_collect` excludes vov_veiled); the linter must NOT couple to it
  - Commits b7c1dbdd (retirement), 5dc8dcc6 (README restructure), b63dc6cf (foundry colophon rbw-h* -> rbw-f*)
  - Absorbed ₢A8AAA D4+D5; D3 (CLAUDE.consumer glossary) landed on 5dc8dcc6, excluded

40c429ddb  ₢BBAA8    Wrap docker login in bounded transient-retry, closing the asymmetry with the curl/poll boundary that already tolerates the same moby#44350 premature-timeout class. docker login is the one login/pull/push verb with no internal retry and a hardcoded non-configurable 15s daemon->registry auth timeout; against a healthy-but-slow GAR backend it fails where the endpoint is fine (the BBAAX gauntlet failure mode). New stateless rbgo_docker_login retries only the surveyed Go-stdlib signature and fails fast on real auth errors, preserving clean-failure semantics; reuses the RBGC HTTP retry budget. Applied at all six foundry login sites (rbfl/rbfr/rbob/rbfd); the depot-regime tripwire (rbndb), a separate dependency cluster that does not reach rbgo, gains the same retry in its own helper sharing only the new RBGC_DOCKER_LOGIN_TRANSIENT_SIGNATURE constant out of rbgc. pull/push left untouched — docker retries them internally.  
efe43240b  ₢BBABP    Emit a periodic 'running <case> (Ns elapsed)' heartbeat to the console while a theurge case blocks, so the operator sees progress during the silent cloud-build poll window. New rbtdre_run_with_heartbeat helper runs the case func on the calling thread (preserving the rbtdrc thread-local context) while a background thread ticks every 30s via mpsc::recv_timeout — exits instantly when the case finishes, so short cases pay no latency. Routed both run_cases and run_single_case through it.

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  Was mechanical ceremony repair; now carries a net-new Rust static-analysis fixture with a real design surface. Three mechanical tracks (ceremony retirement cleanup, claimed-done fixes, verification sweep) frame one design-laden track: a BCG command-compliance linter in theurge. The linter needs judgment; the rest is mechanical. The locked decisions below are settled — do not relitigate them; mount-time minting of the new module/fixture name is the open naming call.
  
  ## Locked decisions (2026-05-31 design conversation, Opus 4.8)
  
  - **Track 2 is a Rust theurge lint, not bash.** The external-command audit becomes a static-analysis fixture in theurge's **fast test suite** — not a bash step in `rbq_qualify_release()`. It rides `rbw-ts.fast` (dev loop) and, via the `complete` suite, `rbw-tr` (release gate), which the ceremony's Step 4 runs pre-strip. That coverage is what lets ceremony **Step 2 be deleted**.
  - **BCG is the single source of truth.** Encode the allowlists from BCG section "Command Dependency Discipline": the POSIX floor, the declared-dependency set (RBS0 inventory + `openssl`), and the **eviction table — enforced**, failing with BCG's prescribed replacement (e.g. `grep` -> `case`/`[[ =~ ]]`). This is stricter than today's Step 2, whose hand-rolled POSIX list wrongly permits evicted commands.
  - **Algorithm: two-pass, function-aware.** Collect every locally-defined function across the corpus; then flag each command-position token not in {bash builtins, local functions, POSIX allowlist, declared deps}, and fail-with-replacement on the eviction table. Soundness rests on presumed BCG-compliance (shellcheck already gates the corpus in `rbq_qualify_fast`) — a command-position scan suffices; no heredoc/case parser.
  - **Checks everything pre-veiling.** Lints ALL `Tools/` bash including `vov_veiled/`. The linter carries **no veil-scope knowledge** — it is a linter, not veiling logic, and must not couple to either packaging path (`vof` kit-parcel collection, or the ceremony strip). The only partition is by **execution environment**: kit-bash (strict BCG) vs GCB-bash (`rbgja/`, `rbgjb/`, `rbgjv/` — looser; gcloud et al. present). **Encode the GCB-domain allowlist now**; do not skip those dirs.
  - **First-run cleanup is in-pace.** Checking veiled bash for the first time may surface real eviction-table violations; fix them in this pace, do not split to a follow-on. Expect the first run not to land green.
  - **Track 4 resolved.** `OPEN_SOURCE_UPSTREAM` pointing at `recipebottle-staging.git` is **intended**. No remote mutation. Ensure no ceremony/doc text asserts the non-staging URL as canonical.
  
  ## Tracks
  
  ### Track 1 — Ceremony retirement cleanup
  
  In `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md`: Step 8 — drop the retired `cp README.consumer.md README.md` (keep the `CLAUDE.consumer.md` copy); the lowercase `readme.md` removal is a no-op (case-insensitive alias of the tracked `README.md`) — replace it with defensive `git rm -f --ignore-unmatch index.html .nojekyll`; Step 10 survival list — remove `.nojekyll` and `index.html` (both retired); staging — keep `git add CLAUDE.md` (Step 8 overwrites it), drop the explicit `README.md` add (`git add -u` covers it). Step 2's dependency-source line vanishes with Step 2 (Track 2).
  
  ### Track 2 — BCG command-lint fixture (locked above)
  
  Build per the locked decisions. Model on the host-side fast-fixture pattern (`rbtdrf_fast.rs`: `dir: &Path -> rbtdre_Verdict`, registered in `rbtdrm_manifest.rs`, added to the `fast` suite). Mint a fresh anchor word — `audit` is taken (image-domain cult verb). Then delete ceremony Step 2.
  
  ### Track 3 — Verify claimed-done
  
  Item B is a FIX: correct the phantom `tt/rbw-tf.QualifyFast.sh` -> `tt/rbw-tq.QualifyFast.sh` in ceremony Step 11 (`rbw-tf` is FixtureRun). Items C (Step 9 marshal-zero ordering before the Step 10 strip) and D (Step 3 regime-completeness check present) verify-and-close.
  
  ### Track 5 — Verification sweep
  
  After Tracks 1-4 land: retirement grep (no live `index.html`/`README.consumer.md`/`.nojekyll` outside retired gallops/memos); foundry colophon grep (no stale `rbw-h*` foundry refs in consumer-visible sources post b63dc6cf); fast qualify `tt/rbw-tq.QualifyFast.sh`; onboarding smoke (`tt/rbw-o.ONBOARDING.sh` + one role track); notch. No auto-wrap — operator decides the close.
  
  ## Verification gates
  
  Each track presented before executing. Review point before committing the ceremony diff. The lint fixture's first full run (including veiled bash) is a discovery step, not a pass gate — surface violations before fixing. No remote mutation. No auto-wrap.
  
  ## References
  
  - `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` — ceremony repair target
  - `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BCG-BashConsoleGuide.md` section "Command Dependency Discipline" — allowlist source of truth (POSIX floor, eviction table, declared-dep principle)
  - `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrf_fast.rs`, `rbtdrm_manifest.rs` — fast-fixture shape + suite/manifest registration
  - `Tools/rbk/rbq_Qualify.sh` — `rbq_qualify_fast`/`rbq_qualify_release`; shellcheck sibling; Step 2 deletion rationale
  - `Tools/vok/vof/` — existing kit-parcel veiling (`vofr_collect` excludes vov_veiled); the linter must NOT couple to it

40c429ddb  ₢BBAA8    Wrap docker login in bounded transient-retry, closing the asymmetry with the curl/poll boundary that already tolerates the same moby#44350 premature-timeout class. docker login is the one login/pull/push verb with no internal retry and a hardcoded non-configurable 15s daemon->registry auth timeout; against a healthy-but-slow GAR backend it fails where the endpoint is fine (the BBAAX gauntlet failure mode). New stateless rbgo_docker_login retries only the surveyed Go-stdlib signature and fails fast on real auth errors, preserving clean-failure semantics; reuses the RBGC HTTP retry budget. Applied at all six foundry login sites (rbfl/rbfr/rbob/rbfd); the depot-regime tripwire (rbndb), a separate dependency cluster that does not reach rbgo, gains the same retry in its own helper sharing only the new RBGC_DOCKER_LOGIN_TRANSIENT_SIGNATURE constant out of rbgc. pull/push left untouched — docker retries them internally.  
efe43240b  ₢BBABP    Emit a periodic 'running <case> (Ns elapsed)' heartbeat to the console while a theurge case blocks, so the operator sees progress during the silent cloud-build poll window. New rbtdre_run_with_heartbeat helper runs the case func on the calling thread (preserving the rbtdrc thread-local context) while a background thread ticks every 30s via mpsc::recv_timeout — exits instantly when the case finishes, so short cases pay no latency. Routed both run_cases and run_single_case through it.

content.text: Let me read the gazette for full paddock and docket content.

content.content:
1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BB
2	
3	## Context
4	
5	Release qualification gap surfaced by the ₣A_ depot-regen heat. Every existing test tier (`rbw-tf` fast, `rbw-tr` release) tolerates accumulated depot state, so silent first-build assumptions slip through. Recent spooks — kludge-aware-charge-prereq (k-prefixed hallmarks at GAR), ZRBOB_PROJECT (compose project name without runtime prefix), rbob_charged_predicate prefix omission, reliquary integrity-broken on negative test — all share a property: they only manifest on first-build paths, and none were caught until live verification surfaced them.
6	
7	This heat constructs `rbw-tP` QualifyPristine — a third tier that **refuses to run** unless marshal-zero state was just committed. The refusal is enforced by the test itself, not by ceremony or operator discipline.
8	
9	## Single operator prerequisite chain
10	
11	Operator has exactly one prerequisite chain: confirm Payor health → marshal-zero → commit → run `rbw-tP`. Payor OAuth is the only credential the operator must have ready; everything else (governor, retriever, director SAs and their RBRA files) is **minted by the qualification itself**, not restored from backup.
12	
13	## Entry contract — load-bearing
14	
15	`rbw-tP` fails-fast unless ALL of the following hold:
16	
17	- Working tree clean
18	- HEAD commit is a marshal-zero commit (detectable signature baked into `rblm_zero` per BBAAB)
19	- RBRR fields are blank (prefixes, depot project ID, etc.)
20	- No RBRA credential files present (governor, retriever, director — all deleted by marshal-zero, recreated by the qualification)
21	- No hallmark fields populated in nameplates
22	- No depot-scoped fields populated in vessel rbrv.env files
23	
24	Operator cannot skip the prerequisite. This is the property that makes the tier catch the silent-first-build bug class by construction.
25	
26	## Failure mode contract
27	
28	Mid-qualification failure means start-over-from-zero, not patch-and-continue. Documented explicitly in runbook to prevent the very accumulated-state bug class this tier exists to catch.
29	
30	## Release-branch execution contract
31	
32	Three properties bind every fixture and phase in this tier.
33	
34	**Run on a release branch.** The release machinery is engineered for branch execution, not main. The branch will accumulate many commits across a successful pristine run. After a failed run, the branch holds bygone commits that `rbw-MZ` does not touch — they remain as history; the next run starts from a fresh marshal-zero commit on top.
35	
36	**Commits during the run are first-class.** Each step that mutates regime state (rbrr.env, rbra files, vessel rbrv.env, etc.) commits the change. Container-recipe work has a documented cognitive failure mode: humans lose track of where they are in long sequences. The growing commit trail is the operator's mental anchor against that.
37	
38	**Stop on very first failure, cleanly and clearly.** A step fails → fixture stops → operator stops → debugging happens immediately on the failed branch. Stderr names the failed step; fixture exits non-zero. Recovery is `rbw-MZ` + retry on a fresh marshal-zero. No graceful degradation, no recovery branches.
39	
40	Engineering scaffolding for any other shape — partial-run cleanup, soft-delete tolerance, multi-mode failure handling, recovery-time orphan inspection, diagnostic-redundancy — is non-load-bearing and does not belong in pristine-tier code. The single mechanical path is the entire surface.
41	
42	## Tier layering
43	
44	Three tiers, escalating cost:
45	
46	| Colophon | Frontispiece | Cost | Entry contract |
47	|----------|--------------|------|----------------|
48	| `rbw-tf` | QualifyFast | seconds | none |
49	| `rbw-tr` | QualifyRelease | minutes | none (accumulated state OK) |
50	| `rbw-tP` | QualifyPristine *(new)* | ~1 hour + cloud $ | marshal-zero just committed |
51	
52	`rbw-tP` is THE release gate. `rbw-tr` becomes the pre-pristine smoke test — cheap-and-frequent for development confidence; pristine for actual release.
53	
54	## Gauntlet test suite — design
55	
56	`rbw-tP` exec's the **gauntlet suite** — a TestSuite (not a custom orchestrator script) composed of fixtures sequenced from marshal-zero state through canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. The TestSuite mechanism is existing infrastructure (`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.{fast,service,crucible,complete}.sh`); the gauntlet suite extends it.
57	
58	**Suite-of-fixtures, not orchestrator-script.** The original construction plan (custom bash orchestrator with inline tabtarget calls) was abandoned in favor of suite composition. Each section of the release-qualification sequence becomes a fixture; the suite composes them in order.
59	
60	**Naming hygiene.** "Gauntlet" is the suite name; "pristine" is reserved for the §1 pristine-lifecycle fixture and informally for tier vocabulary. The suite name does not appear at fixture or case level — distinct stratification across naming layers (suite ≠ fixture ≠ case).
61	
62	**State ladder, fixtures along it.** The suite walks a state ladder no single fixture covers:
63	
64	1. enrollment-validation (preflight, state-indifferent)
65	2. pristine-lifecycle (§1: marshal-zero gate + throwaway depot/SA lifecycle)
66	3. canonical-establish (§2: canonical depot levy + governor mantle + retriever invest + director invest + IAM propagation wait)
67	4. canonical-onboarding-sequence (§3: reliquary inscribe + per-vessel hallmark ordain — handbook-driven build walks per BBAAf)
68	5. regime-validation, regime-smoke (post-§3, regimes now populated)
69	6. four-mode (cloud-build mode coverage)
70	7. tadmor, moriah, srjcl, pluml (§4: per-crucible test fixtures, vessel-exists preconditions per BBAAs)
71	
72	No automatic teardown — see "No-teardown decision" below.
73	
74	## Dual purpose: handbook-regression coverage
75	
76	The gauntlet's primary purpose is release qualification (catch silent first-build assumptions). Its **secondary purpose** is automated handbook-regression coverage: ensure operator-facing onboarding handbook tracks (`rbh*`) stay in sync with working code so future operators following the prescribed steps aren't surprised by drift.
77	
78	Build-side handbook coverage lives in BBAAf (canonical-onboarding-sequence): each ordain case walks the corresponding director-mode handbook track (`rbw-Odf` conjure / `rbw-Oda` airgap / `rbw-Odb` bind / `rbw-Odg` graft) rather than calling inscribe/ordain machinery directly.
79	
80	**Build/run split — load-bearing.** Handbook walks cover the BUILD step (vessel construction) and stop when the hallmark lands in GAR. Run-side fixtures (per-crucible: tadmor/moriah/srjcl/pluml) stay as direct test fixtures with vessel-exists preconditions, NOT handbook walks. The split keeps run-side reusable against any vessel source — coupling them to handbook prose breaks reusability.
81	
82	Drift between fixtures and handbooks is accepted future maintenance: whoever changes one is responsible for updating the other; the gauntlet's job is to surface drift, not prevent it by construction.
83	
84	Run-side handbook coverage (`rbw-Ots`, the rack portion of `rbw-Ofc`) is explicitly out of scope for this heat.
85	
86	## Fixture disposition — Independent vs StateProgressing
87	
88	Each fixture declares a disposition flag in Rust, controlling engine behavior:
89	
90	- **Independent** — cases are self-contained; suite-order is informational. Engine permits keep-going mode for surveying.
91	- **StateProgressing** — case N's success establishes preconditions for case N+1. Engine refuses keep-going mode (incoherent: failed case leaves broken precondition). Fail-fast required.
92	
93	**Default is Independent.** Existing fixtures (`enrollment-validation`, `regime-validation`, `regime-smoke`, `four-mode`, `tadmor`, `moriah`, `srjcl`, `pluml`) are Independent and require no tagging change. Only fixtures whose case-N success establishes case-N+1 preconditions get StateProgressing — currently `pristine-lifecycle`, `canonical-establish`, and `canonical-onboarding-sequence`.
94	
95	Suite-level disposition derives from membership: any StateProgressing fixture in the suite makes the suite state-progressing → suite-level keep-going refused.
96	
97	**Per-case precondition probes.** Each case in a StateProgressing fixture probes its precondition at start. If state matches expectation, run. If not, refuse with operator-actionable diagnostic naming the expected state and the closest fixture that produces it. This enables safe a-la-carte single-case rerun — the engine doesn't need to know the case's history; the case's probe enforces.
98	
99	## Suite-level fail-fast
100	
101	Across all suites — not just gauntlet — any fixture failure stops subsequent fixtures from running automatically. State-progressing suites require this for correctness; independent suites adopt it for the simpler mental model. Operators who want full surveying run individual fixtures separately.
102	
103	## No-teardown decision
104	
105	The gauntlet suite omits an automatic teardown phase. After green tally, the canonical depot, SAs, hallmarks, and RBRA files persist for operator inspection. Deliberate trade-off:
106	
107	- **Inspect-after-success** is genuine operator value — browse canonical depot in GCP console, inspect GAR contents, run ad-hoc verification a test sequence cannot anticipate.
108	- **Cleanup is operator-driven** — manual sequence is `rbw-fA` per vessel + `rbw-arD` + `rbw-adD` + `rbw-dU` (governor cleanup folds into unmake per BBAAN). All existing tabtargets; no new infrastructure.
109	- **Failed runs don't accumulate cleanup debt** — under fail-fast, teardown wouldn't run anyway; the no-teardown decision only changes behavior on full success.
110	- **Cost visibility** — pending-delete soft-deleted projects accumulate per run (RELEASE.md accepts this); not changed by teardown decision.
111	
112	Documentation of the post-success cleanup ceremony is BBAAI work (RELEASE.md).
113	
114	## Operator scope
115	
116	Single operator (project lead). Not designed for multi-operator workflow. The README.md release section remains the runbook for the gauntlet itself; the dual-purpose handbook-regression coverage protects the broader operator surface (onboarding tracks under `rbh*`) from drift.
117	
118	## Coupling to ₣A_
119	
120	Cutover work from ₣A_ informing `rbw-tP`'s sequence has landed (BBAAM depot-identity-collapse). ₣A_'s remaining paces run independently of BB.
121	
122	## Locked design decisions
123	
124	- **Depot identity collapse**: RBRR collapses to `RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER`; `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` flows through depot-affiliated resources; per-moniker depot fact-files; pristine-fixture moniker autodetect lives in Rust (`rbtdrp_pristine.rs`), not payor.
125	- **Cult-verb naming**: SA domain muster→**roster**; image domain muster→**audit**. Domain-exclusive split; lowercase tail letters preserved for no-cloud-change observation colophons.
126	- **Build/run split**: handbook walks cover build only; per-crucible fixtures stay reusable as direct tests.
127	
128	## Calibrant fixture family — BBAAh
129	
130	Synthetic Rust fixtures with deterministic verdicts, registered in the manifest by BBAAh. The family exists to give ₣BC `rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test` deterministic targets for bash-blackbox driving and manifest-coverage validation; here in ₣BB only the foundation lands.
131	
132	**Anchor word: `calibrant`.** Module `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` (classifier `l` for caLibrant since `c` is owned by crucible). Replaces the dormant `rbtdrd_dummy.rs` placeholder. Test counterpart `rbtdtl_calibrant.rs`. Internal-only vocabulary; not end-user-facing.
133	
134	**Fixture catalog** (Rust, manifest-registered):
135	
136	- `calibrant-verdicts` (Independent) — pass / fail / skip / pass_with_output. Verdict-path coverage. The case-written file (`output.txt`) is distinct from the engine-auto `trace.txt`.
137	- `calibrant-fail-fast` (Independent, multi-section) — fixture-internal fail-fast across cases and across sections; "not_reached" cases write sentinel files used as absence-of-side-effect assertions.
138	- `calibrant-progressing` (StateProgressing) — exercises `rbtdrb_Probe` diagnostic format and `rbtdre_resolve_fail_fast` policy gate.
139	- `calibrant-sentinel` (Independent) — single case writing a known file when it runs. Used for suite-level fail-fast verification.
140	
141	Suite: `calibrant` = [verdicts, fail-fast, sentinel]. Sentinel-after-fail verifies cross-fixture fail-fast contract.
142	
143	Downstream consumption — bash testbench, manifest-coverage runtime checks, coverage-validation fixtures and driver — lives in ₣BC.
144	
145	## References
146	
147	- ₣A_ rbk-mvp-3-resource-prefix-and-depot-regen — surfaced the gap
148	- ₣BC rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test — calibrant downstream consumers
149	- `tt/rbw-tr.QualifyRelease.sh` — current release qualify; layered alongside, not replaced
150	- `Tools/rbk/rblm_cli.sh` — `rbw-MZ` zeroes local regime; marshal-zero signature baked here per BBAAB

It rides `rbw-ts.fast` (dev loop) and, via the `complete` suite, `rbw-tr` (release gate), which the ceremony's Step 4 runs pre-strip. That coverage is what lets ceremony **Step 2 be deleted**.
162	- **BCG is the single source of truth.** Encode the allowlists from BCG section "Command Dependency Discipline": the POSIX floor, the declared-dependency set (RBS0 inventory + `openssl`), and the **eviction table — enforced**, failing with BCG's prescribed replacement (e.g. `grep` -> `case`/`[[ =~ ]]`). # jjezs_paddock ₣BB

## Context

Release qualification gap surfaced by the ₣A_ depot-regen heat. Every existing test tier (`rbw-tf` fast, `rbw-tr` release) tolerates accumulated depot state, so silent first-build assumptions slip through. Recent spooks — kludge-aware-charge-prereq (k-prefixed hallmarks at GAR), ZRBOB_PROJECT (compose project name without runtime prefix), rbob_charged_predicate prefix omission, reliquary integrity-broken on negative test — all share a property: they only manifest on first-build paths, and none were caught until live verification surfaced them.

This heat constructs `rbw-tP` QualifyPristine — a third tier that **refuses to run** unless marshal-zero state was just committed. The refusal is enforced by the test itself, not by ceremony or operator discipline.

## Single operator prerequisite chain

Operator has exactly one prerequisite chain: confirm Payor health → marshal-zero → commit → run `rbw-tP`. Payor OAuth is the only credential the operator must have ready; everything else (governor, retriever, director SAs and their RBRA files) is **minted by the qualification itself**, not restored from backup.

## Entry contract — load-bearing

`rbw-tP` fails-fast unless ALL of the following hold:

- Working tree clean
- HEAD commit is a marshal-zero commit (detectable signature baked into `rblm_zero` per BBAAB)
- RBRR fields are blank (prefixes, depot project ID, etc.)
- No RBRA credential files present (governor, retriever, director — all deleted by marshal-zero, recreated by the qualification)
- No hallmark fields populated in nameplates
- No depot-scoped fields populated in vessel rbrv.env files

Operator cannot skip the prerequisite. This is the property that makes the tier catch the silent-first-build bug class by construction.

## Failure mode contract

Mid-qualification failure means start-over-from-zero, not patch-and-continue. Documented explicitly in runbook to prevent the very accumulated-state bug class this tier exists to catch.

## Release-branch execution contract

Three properties bind every fixture and phase in this tier.

**Run on a release branch.** The release machinery is engineered for branch execution, not main. The branch will accumulate many commits across a successful pristine run. After a failed run, the branch holds bygone commits that `rbw-MZ` does not touch — they remain as history; the next run starts from a fresh marshal-zero commit on top.

**Commits during the run are first-class.** Each step that mutates regime state (rbrr.env, rbra files, vessel rbrv.env, etc.) commits the change. Container-recipe work has a documented cognitive failure mode: humans lose track of where they are in long sequences. The growing commit trail is the operator's mental anchor against that.

**Stop on very first failure, cleanly and clearly.** A step fails → fixture stops → operator stops → debugging happens immediately on the failed branch. Stderr names the failed step; fixture exits non-zero. Recovery is `rbw-MZ` + retry on a fresh marshal-zero. No graceful degradation, no recovery branches.

Engineering scaffolding for any other shape — partial-run cleanup, soft-delete tolerance, multi-mode failure handling, recovery-time orphan inspection, diagnostic-redundancy — is non-load-bearing and does not belong in pristine-tier code. The single mechanical path is the entire surface.

## Tier layering

Three tiers, escalating cost:

| Colophon | Frontispiece | Cost | Entry contract |
|----------|--------------|------|----------------|
| `rbw-tf` | QualifyFast | seconds | none |
| `rbw-tr` | QualifyRelease | minutes | none (accumulated state OK) |
| `rbw-tP` | QualifyPristine *(new)* | ~1 hour + cloud $ | marshal-zero just committed |

`rbw-tP` is THE release gate. `rbw-tr` becomes the pre-pristine smoke test — cheap-and-frequent for development confidence; pristine for actual release.

## Gauntlet test suite — design

`rbw-tP` exec's the **gauntlet suite** — a TestSuite (not a custom orchestrator script) composed of fixtures sequenced from marshal-zero state through canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. The TestSuite mechanism is existing infrastructure (`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.{fast,service,crucible,complete}.sh`); the gauntlet suite extends it.

**Suite-of-fixtures, not orchestrator-script.** The original construction plan (custom bash orchestrator with inline tabtarget calls) was abandoned in favor of suite composition. Each section of the release-qualification sequence becomes a fixture; the suite composes them in order.

**Naming hygiene.** "Gauntlet" is the suite name; "pristine" is reserved for the §1 pristine-lifecycle fixture and informally for tier vocabulary. The suite name does not appear at fixture or case level — distinct stratification across naming layers (suite ≠ fixture ≠ case).

**State ladder, fixtures along it.** The suite walks a state ladder no single fixture covers:

1. enrollment-validation (preflight, state-indifferent)
2. pristine-lifecycle (§1: marshal-zero gate + throwaway depot/SA lifecycle)
3. canonical-establish (§2: canonical depot levy + governor mantle + retriever invest + director invest + IAM propagation wait)
4. canonical-onboarding-sequence (§3: reliquary inscribe + per-vessel hallmark ordain — handbook-driven build walks per BBAAf)
5. regime-validation, regime-smoke (post-§3, regimes now populated)
6. four-mode (cloud-build mode coverage)
7. tadmor, moriah, srjcl, pluml (§4: per-crucible test fixtures, vessel-exists preconditions per BBAAs)

No automatic teardown — see "No-teardown decision" below.

## Dual purpose: handbook-regression coverage

The gauntlet's primary purpose is release qualification (catch silent first-build assumptions). Its **secondary purpose** is automated handbook-regression coverage: ensure operator-facing onboarding handbook tracks (`rbh*`) stay in sync with working code so future operators following the prescribed steps aren't surprised by drift.

Build-side handbook coverage lives in BBAAf (canonical-onboarding-sequence): each ordain case walks the corresponding director-mode handbook track (`rbw-Odf` conjure / `rbw-Oda` airgap / `rbw-Odb` bind / `rbw-Odg` graft) rather than calling inscribe/ordain machinery directly.

**Build/run split — load-bearing.** Handbook walks cover the BUILD step (vessel construction) and stop when the hallmark lands in GAR. Run-side fixtures (per-crucible: tadmor/moriah/srjcl/pluml) stay as direct test fixtures with vessel-exists preconditions, NOT handbook walks. The split keeps run-side reusable against any vessel source — coupling them to handbook prose breaks reusability.

Drift between fixtures and handbooks is accepted future maintenance: whoever changes one is responsible for updating the other; the gauntlet's job is to surface drift, not prevent it by construction.

Run-side handbook coverage (`rbw-Ots`, the rack portion of `rbw-Ofc`) is explicitly out of scope for this heat.

## Fixture disposition — Independent vs StateProgressing

Each fixture declares a disposition flag in Rust, controlling engine behavior:

- **Independent** — cases are self-contained; suite-order is informational. Engine permits keep-going mode for surveying.
- **StateProgressing** — case N's success establishes preconditions for case N+1. Engine refuses keep-going mode (incoherent: failed case leaves broken precondition). Fail-fast required.

**Default is Independent.** Existing fixtures (`enrollment-validation`, `regime-validation`, `regime-smoke`, `four-mode`, `tadmor`, `moriah`, `srjcl`, `pluml`) are Independent and require no tagging change. Only fixtures whose case-N success establishes case-N+1 preconditions get StateProgressing — currently `pristine-lifecycle`, `canonical-establish`, and `canonical-onboarding-sequence`.

Suite-level disposition derives from membership: any StateProgressing fixture in the suite makes the suite state-progressing → suite-level keep-going refused.

**Per-case precondition probes.** Each case in a StateProgressing fixture probes its precondition at start. If state matches expectation, run. If not, refuse with operator-actionable diagnostic naming the expected state and the closest fixture that produces it. This enables safe a-la-carte single-case rerun — the engine doesn't need to know the case's history; the case's probe enforces.

## Suite-level fail-fast

Across all suites — not just gauntlet — any fixture failure stops subsequent fixtures from running automatically. State-progressing suites require this for correctness; independent suites adopt it for the simpler mental model. Operators who want full surveying run individual fixtures separately.

## No-teardown decision

The gauntlet suite omits an automatic teardown phase. After green tally, the canonical depot, SAs, hallmarks, and RBRA files persist for operator inspection. Deliberate trade-off:

- **Inspect-after-success** is genuine operator value — browse canonical depot in GCP console, inspect GAR contents, run ad-hoc verification a test sequence cannot anticipate.
- **Cleanup is operator-driven** — manual sequence is `rbw-fA` per vessel + `rbw-arD` + `rbw-adD` + `rbw-dU` (governor cleanup folds into unmake per BBAAN). All existing tabtargets; no new infrastructure.
- **Failed runs don't accumulate cleanup debt** — under fail-fast, teardown wouldn't run anyway; the no-teardown decision only changes behavior on full success.
- **Cost visibility** — pending-delete soft-deleted projects accumulate per run (RELEASE.md accepts this); not changed by teardown decision.

Documentation of the post-success cleanup ceremony is BBAAI work (RELEASE.md).

## Operator scope

Single operator (project lead). Not designed for multi-operator workflow. The README.md release section remains the runbook for the gauntlet itself; the dual-purpose handbook-regression coverage protects the broader operator surface (onboarding tracks under `rbh*`) from drift.

## Coupling to ₣A_

Cutover work from ₣A_ informing `rbw-tP`'s sequence has landed (BBAAM depot-identity-collapse). ₣A_'s remaining paces run independently of BB.

## Locked design decisions

- **Depot identity collapse**: RBRR collapses to `RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER`; `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` flows through depot-affiliated resources; per-moniker depot fact-files; pristine-fixture moniker autodetect lives in Rust (`rbtdrp_pristine.rs`), not payor.
- **Cult-verb naming**: SA domain muster→**roster**; image domain muster→**audit**. Domain-exclusive split; lowercase tail letters preserved for no-cloud-change observation colophons.
- **Build/run split**: handbook walks cover build only; per-crucible fixtures stay reusable as direct tests.

## Calibrant fixture family — BBAAh

Synthetic Rust fixtures with deterministic verdicts, registered in the manifest by BBAAh. The family exists to give ₣BC `rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test` deterministic targets for bash-blackbox driving and manifest-coverage validation; here in ₣BB only the foundation lands.

**Anchor word: `calibrant`.** Module `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` (classifier `l` for caLibrant since `c` is owned by crucible). Replaces the dormant `rbtdrd_dummy.rs` placeholder. Test counterpart `rbtdtl_calibrant.rs`. Internal-only vocabulary; not end-user-facing.

**Fixture catalog** (Rust, manifest-registered):

- `calibrant-verdicts` (Independent) — pass / fail / skip / pass_with_output. Verdict-path coverage. The case-written file (`output.txt`) is distinct from the engine-auto `trace.txt`.
- `calibrant-fail-fast` (Independent, multi-section) — fixture-internal fail-fast across cases and across sections; "not_reached" cases write sentinel files used as absence-of-side-effect assertions.
- `calibrant-progressing` (StateProgressing) — exercises `rbtdrb_Probe` diagnostic format and `rbtdre_resolve_fail_fast` policy gate.
- `calibrant-sentinel` (Independent) — single case writing a known file when it runs. Used for suite-level fail-fast verification.

Suite: `calibrant` = [verdicts, fail-fast, sentinel]. Sentinel-after-fail verifies cross-fixture fail-fast contract.

Downstream consumption — bash testbench, manifest-coverage runtime checks, coverage-validation fixtures and driver — lives in ₣BC.

## References

- ₣A_ rbk-mvp-3-resource-prefix-and-depot-regen — surfaced the gap
- ₣BC rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test — calibrant downstream consumers
- `tt/rbw-tr.QualifyRelease.sh` — current release qualify; layered alongside, not replaced
- `Tools/rbk/rblm_cli.sh` — `rbw-MZ` zeroes local regime; marshal-zero signature baked here per BBAAB
- `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` — upstream contribution ceremony; pristine-pass becomes a precondition (BBAAH)

content.text:
Starting from a clean tree on `main`, fresh session, officium `☉260531-1001` open. Heat ₣BB is at 58/68 paces. Here's the mount.

## Next pace: ₢BBABM (release-ceremony-and-bcg-command-lint)

**Goal in one sentence:** Repair the upstream release-prep ceremony's stale steps *and* replace its hand-rolled bash external-command audit (Step 2) with a real Rust BCG-command-compliance linter living as a fast-suite fixture in theurge — then delete Step 2 once the linter covers it.

**Heat context:** ₣BB — rbk-15-mvp-release-qualification. Building `rbw-tP` QualifyPristine, a marshal-zero-gated release tier that refuses to run on accumulated state. Recent work: docker-login transient-retry (₢BBAA8), theurge case heartbeat (₢BBABP).

**Name assessment:** Silks `release-ceremony-and-bcg-command-lint` fits the docket cleanly. No rename needed.

## The shape — four tracks, one with teeth

| Track | Character | Work |
|-------|-----------|------|
| **1 — Ceremony retirement cleanup** | mechanical | Edit `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md`: drop retired `cp README.consumer.md README.md` in Step 8, swap the no-op lowercase `readme.md` removal for defensive `git rm -f --ignore-unmatch index.html .nojekyll`, prune `.nojekyll`/`index.html` from Step 10 survival list, simplify staging. |
| **2 — BCG command-lint fixture** | **design-laden** | Net-new Rust static-analysis fixture in theurge's fast suite. Two-pass function-aware command-position scan against BCG's allowlists (POSIX floor + declared deps + **enforced eviction table**). Kit-bash strict / GCB-bash looser partition. Then delete ceremony Step 2. |
| **3 — Verify claimed-done** | mechanical | Fix phantom `rbw-tf.QualifyFast.sh` → `rbw-tq.QualifyFast.sh` in Step 11; verify-and-close items C (Step 9 ordering) and D (Step 3 regime check). |
| **5 — Verification sweep** | mechanical | After 1–4 land: retirement grep, foundry colophon grep, fast qualify, onboarding smoke, notch. |

(Track 4 is already resolved in the locked decisions — `OPEN_SOURCE_UPSTREAM` → staging is intended; just ensure no doc asserts otherwise.)

## Execution strategy

**Sequential, opus-tier.** Track 2 is the load-bearing design work — a static analyzer with a real judgment surface (command-position detection, allowlist encoding, the kit-vs-GCB partition). It also gates Step 2's deletion, so it anchors the order: **Track 2 → Track 1 → Track 3 → Track 5**. The tracks touch overlapping files (the ceremony `.md`, theurge Rust), so no parallelism.

The docket sets two hard postures I'll honor: **each track presented before executing**, and **the linter's first full run over veiled bash is a discovery step, not a pass gate** — I'll surface violations before fixing, and the first run is expected not to land green.

## Two things before I start

The docket flags one open decision and one I want to confirm:

1. **Open naming call (mount-time mint):** Track 2 needs a fresh anchor word for the new module/fixture. `audit` is taken (image-domain cult verb). The fixture follows `rbtdrf_fast.rs`'s shape and registers in `rbtdrm_manifest.rs`. I have candidates in mind but want your input.

2. **Start point:** I'd open on Track 2 (build linter, present, then delete Step 2), since the mechanical ceremony edits in Track 1 partly depend on Step 2 being gone.

Let me get your read on the name and confirm the start order before I touch anything.

questions.question: Track 2 needs a fresh anchor word for the new theurge lint module/fixture. It's a BCG command-compliance linter (scans command-position tokens against allowlists). `audit` is taken (image-domain cult verb). Which direction?

options.label: vet
options.description: rbtdrv_vet.rs / vet-commands fixture. Short, evokes scrutiny/clearance. Classifier 'v'.

options.label: warrant
options.description: rbtdrw_warrant.rs / warrant fixture. Evokes a compliance check granting permission. Classifier 'w'.

options.label: lint
options.description: rbtdrl_lint... but 'l' classifier is owned by calibrant. Would need another letter. Plain/literal.

options.label: sentry / proscribe
options.description: Other security-flavored verbs — I can propose a slate if none above fit.

options.label: Track 2 first

content.text:
Good call — I'll mint something distinctive and leave the common security words free for future RBK-adjacent kits. Track 2 first, confirmed.

Theurge already runs an alchemical/metallurgical vocabulary — `crucible`, `calibrant`, and `assay` (the latter taken over in APCK). A linter that judges bash against the BCG standard *is* a purity test against a reference, so the metallurgy family has a near-perfect fanciful word. Let me check which classifier letters are free before I commit.

content.text:
The clean pick is **`cupel`** (classifier `u` — c**u**pel; `c`, `p`, `e`, `l` are all taken, `u` is free on both `rbtdru_`/`rbtdtu_` sides).

Why it fits and stays out of everyone's way:
- **Fanciful + globally unique** — a cupel is the bone-ash assay vessel used in *cupellation*, where molten lead carries off base metals and leaves the pure noble metal behind. Nobody else's kit is going to reach for it.
- **Stays in theurge's metallurgy family** — sits naturally beside `crucible` and `calibrant`, and beside `assay` (which you've spent in APCK, so reusing the literal word would collide — `cupel` is the *apparatus*, not the act).
- **Semantically it's the fixture's job, not BCG's** — the cupel is where dross is driven off and purity remains. The linter drives off non-BCG-compliant commands and leaves clean bash. (A "touchstone" would name the *reference standard* — that's BCG itself, not the test.)

So: module `rbtdru_cupel.rs`, test `rbtdtu_cupel.rs`, fixture `cupel` registered in `rbtdrm_manifest.rs`, added to the `fast` suite. If `cupel` rubs you wrong, say so now — otherwise I'll build on it.

Let me set up tracking and start reading the Track 2 source-of-truth (BCG) and the fixture pattern.
I'll create the track tasks, then start reading the Track 2 sources in parallel.

Partition kit-bash (strict) vs GCB-bash (rbgja/rbgjb/rbgjv, looser). Register in rbtdrm_manifest.rs, add to fast suite. First run over veiled bash is discovery — surface violations, then fix in-pace. Then delete ceremony Step 2.

78	// ── Helpers ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
79	
80	/// Sub-assertion within a case: run bash snippet, check exit code.
81	struct RbtdrfSub {
82	    label: &'static str,
83	    setup: &'static str,
84	    command: &'static str,
85	    expect_ok: bool,
86	}
87	
88	impl RbtdrfSub {
89	    const fn ok(label: &'static str, setup: &'static str) -> Self {
90	        Self { label, setup, command: "buv_vet \"TEST\"", expect_ok: true }
91	    }
92	    const fn fatal(label: &'static str, setup: &'static str) -> Self {
93	        Self { label, setup, command: "buv_vet \"TEST\"", expect_ok: false }
94	    }
95	    const fn ok_cmd(label: &'static str, setup: &'static str, command: &'static str) -> Self {
96	        Self { label, setup, command, expect_ok: true }
97	    }
98	    const fn fatal_cmd(label: &'static str, setup: &'static str, command: &'static str) -> Self {
99	        Self { label, setup, command, expect_ok: false }
100	    }
101	}
102	
103	/// Run a bash script, return (exit_code, stdout, stderr). Saves traces to case dir.
104	fn rbtdrf_run_bash(
105	    project_root: &Path,
106	    script: &str,
107	    dir: &Path,
108	    trace_prefix: &str,
109	) -> Result<(i32, String, String), String> {
110	    let output = Command::new("bash")
111	        .arg("-c")
112	        .arg(script)
113	        .current_dir(project_root)
114	        .output()
115	        .map_err(|e| format!("bash execution failed: {}", e))?;
116	
117	    let stdout = String::from_utf8_lossy(&output.stdout).into_owned();
118	    let stderr = String::from_utf8_lossy(&output.stderr).into_owned();
119	    let code = output.status.code().unwrap_or(-1);
120	
121	    let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join(format!("{}-script.sh", trace_prefix)), script);
122	    let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join(format!("{}-stdout.txt", trace_prefix)), &stdout);
123	    let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join(format!("{}-stderr.txt", trace_prefix)), &stderr);
124	
125	    Ok((code, stdout, stderr))
126	}
127	
128	/// Run enrollment-validation sub-assertions against BUV.
129	fn rbtdrf_run_ev(
130	    dir: &Path,
131	    enrollment: &str,
132	    subs: &[RbtdrfSub],
133	) -> rbtdre_Verdict {
134	    let root = match std::env::current_dir() {
135	        Ok(r) => r,
136	        Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("cannot get cwd: {}", e)),
137	    };
138	    let buv = root.join("Tools/buk/buv_validation.sh");
139	
140	    for (i, sub) in subs.iter().enumerate() {
141	        let script = format!(
142	            "set -euo pipefail\nsource '{}'\nzbuv_kindle\nzbuv_reset_enrollment\n{}\n{}\n{}",
143	            rbtdrx_native_to_posix(&buv),
144	            enrollment,
145	            sub.setup,
146	            sub.command,
147	        );
148	
149	        match rbtdrf_run_bash(&root, &script, dir, &format!("sub-{}", i)) {
150	            Ok((code, _, _)) => {
151	                let ok = code == 0;
152	                if ok != sub.expect_ok {
153	                    return if sub.expect_ok {
154	                        rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("{}: expected ok, got exit {}", sub.label, code))
155	                    } else {
156	                        rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("{}: expected failure, got exit 0", sub.label))
157	                    };
158	                }
159	            }
160	            Err(e) => {
161	                return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("{}: {}", sub.label, e));
162	            }
163	        }
164	    }
165	    rbtdre_Verdict::Pass
166	}
167	
168	/// Drive a regime's public `*_probate` function against a synthetic
169	/// baseline+override written to the case temp dir. `expect_ok` selects the
170	/// verdict polarity: pass-anchors validate the pristine baseline (true);
171	/// negatives apply one violating override and expect non-zero (false).
172	/// Sources only the contract surface — `buv_validation.sh` plus the one regime
173	/// module — mirroring the `buv_vet` bridge that enrollment-validation uses: no
174	/// internal-module chain, no z-private reach.
175	///
176	/// `tools_subdir` is the repo-relative directory holding the module
177	/// ("Tools/rbk" or "Tools/buk"). `prelude` is raw bash staged after buv's
178	/// kindle but before the regime module's probate — for regimes whose kindle or
179	/// enforce reaches a neighbor (rbrp → rbgc's payor regex; burs → bubc, whose
180	/// constants its enrollment help-string expands). Relative paths resolve
181	/// against cwd (the repo root, set by rbtdrf_run_bash). Empty for the common
182	/// case, mirroring how rbtdrf_rs_rbrr_nonempty_prefix stages rbgc in-harness.
183	fn rbtdrf_run_probate_in(
184	    dir: &Path,
185	    tools_subdir: &str,
186	    module: &str,
187	    probate_fn: &str,
188	    prelude: &str,
189	    baseline: &str,
190	    override_: &str,
191	    expect_ok: bool,
192	    label: &str,
193	) -> rbtdre_Verdict {
194	    let root = match std::env::current_dir() {
195	        Ok(r) => r,
196	        Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("cannot get cwd: {}", e)),
197	    };
198	    let buv = root.join("Tools/buk/buv_validation.sh");
199	    let module_path = root.join(tools_subdir).join(module);
200	
201	    let env_file = dir.join(format!("{}.env", label));
202	    let body = format!("{}\n{}\n", baseline, override_);
203	    if let Err(e) = std::fs::write(&env_file, &body) {
204	        return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("{}: write env failed: {}", label, e));
205	    }
206	
207	    let script = format!(
208	        "set -euo pipefail\n\
209	         source '{}'\n\
210	         source '{}'\n\
211	         zbuv_kindle\n\
212	         {}\
213	         {} '{}'",
214	        rbtdrx_native_to_posix(&buv),
215	        rbtdrx_native_to_posix(&module_path),
216	        prelude,
217	        probate_fn,
218	        rbtdrx_native_to_posix(&env_file),
219	    );
220	
221	    match rbtdrf_run_bash(&root, &script, dir, label) {
222	        Ok((code, _, _)) => {
223	            let ok = code == 0;
224	            if ok == expect_ok {
225	                rbtdre_Verdict::Pass
226	            } else if expect_ok {
227	                rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("{}: expected ok, got exit {}", label, code))
228	            } else {
229	                rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("{}: expected failure, got exit 0", label))
230	            }
231	        }
232	        Err(e) => rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("{}: {}", label, e)),
233	    }
234	}
235	
236	/// Common-case wrapper: RBK module, no prereqs. Preserves the call shape used
237	/// by the reference rv_ quartet (rbrr/rbrd/rbrv/rbrn).
238	fn rbtdrf_run_probate(
239	    dir: &Path,
240	    module: &str,
241	    probate_fn: &str,
242	    baseline: &str,
243	    override_: &str,
244	    expect_ok: bool,
245	    label: &str,
246	) -> rbtdre_Verdict {
247	    rbtdrf_run_probate_in(
248	        dir, "Tools/rbk", module, probate_fn, "", baseline, override_, expect_ok, label,
249	    )
250	}

252	/// Run a tabtarget and check exit 0.
253	fn rbtdrf_run_tt(
254	    project_root: &Path,
255	    colophon: &str,
256	    args: &[&str],
257	    dir: &Path,
258	    label: &str,
259	) -> Result<(), String> {
260	    let tt = rbtdri_find_tabtarget_global(project_root, colophon)?;
261	    let output = Command::new(&tt)
262	        .args(args)
263	        .current_dir(project_root)
264	        .output()
265	        .map_err(|e| format!("{}: failed to run {}: {}", label, tt.display(), e))?;
266	
267	    let code = output.status.code().unwrap_or(-1);
268	    let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join(format!("{}-stdout.txt", label)), &output.stdout);
269	    let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join(format!("{}-stderr.txt", label)), &output.stderr);
270	
271	    if code != 0 {
272	        return Err(format!(
273	            "{}: {} exited {} — {}",
274	            label,
275	            colophon,
276	            code,
277	            String::from_utf8_lossy(&output.stderr),
278	        ));
279	    }
280	    Ok(())
281	}
282	
283	/// Run a tabtarget that is expected to fail. Inverts rbtdrf_run_tt:
284	/// non-zero exit returns Ok(()), zero exit returns Err. Invocation errors
285	/// (tabtarget not found, launcher failure) still propagate as Err.
286	fn rbtdrf_run_tt_neg(
287	    project_root: &Path,
288	    colophon: &str,
289	    args: &[&str],
290	    dir: &Path,
291	    label: &str,
292	) -> Result<(), String> {
293	    let tt = rbtdri_find_tabtarget_global(project_root, colophon)?;
294	    let output = Command::new(&tt)
295	        .args(args)
296	        .current_dir(project_root)
297	        .output()
298	        .map_err(|e| format!("{}: failed to run {}: {}", label, tt.display(), e))?;
299	
300	    let code = output.status.code().unwrap_or(-1);
301	    let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join(format!("{}-stdout.txt", label)), &output.stdout);
302	    let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join(format!("{}-stderr.txt", label)), &output.stderr);
303	
304	    if code == 0 {
305	        return Err(format!(
306	            "{}: {} expected failure, got exit 0",
307	            label, colophon,
308	        ));
309	    }
310	    Ok(())
311	}

567	// --- Reference types ---
568	
569	const RBTDRF_VALID_DIGEST: &str =
570	    "sha256:abcdef0123456789abcdef0123456789abcdef0123456789abcdef0123456789";
571	
572	fn rbtdrf_ev_odref_valid(dir: &Path) -> rbtdre_Verdict {
573	    let root = match std::env::current_dir() {
574	        Ok(r) => r,
575	        Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("cannot get cwd: {}", e)),
576	    };
577	    let buv = root.join("Tools/buk/buv_validation.sh");
578	    let enrollment = format!(
579	        "set -euo pipefail\nsource '{}'\nzbuv_kindle\nzbuv_reset_enrollment\n\
580	         buv_regime_enroll \"TEST\"\nbuv_group_enroll \"References\"\n\
581	         buv_odref_enroll TEST_IMAGE \"Container image\"",
582	        rbtdrx_native_to_posix(&buv),
583	    );
584	    let d = RBTDRF_VALID_DIGEST;
585	
586	    let subs: &[(&str, &str)] = &[
587	        ("standard registry", &format!("docker.io/library/alpine@{}", d)),
588	        ("multi-level repo", &format!("us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/my-proj/my-repo/tool@{}", d)),
589	        ("registry with port", &format!("registry.local:5000/myimage@{}", d)),
590	    ];
591	
592	    for (i, (label, image)) in subs.iter().enumerate() {
593	        let script = format!("{}\nexport TEST_IMAGE=\"{}\"\nbuv_vet \"TEST\"", enrollment, image);
594	        match rbtdrf_run_bash(&root, &script, dir, &format!("sub-{}", i)) {
595	            Ok((0, _, _)) => {}
596	            Ok((code, _, _)) => {
597	                return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("{}: expected ok, got exit {}", label, code));
598	            }
599	            Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("{}: {}", label, e)),
600	        }
601	    }
602	    rbtdre_Verdict::Pass
603	}
604

608	         buv_odref_enroll TEST_IMAGE \"Container image\"",
609	        &[RbtdrfSub::fatal("tag only",
610	            "export TEST_IMAGE=\"docker.io/library/alpine:latest\"")],
611	    )
612	}

824	    rbtdrf_run_ev(dir,
825	        "buv_regime_enroll \"TEST\"\nbuv_group_enroll \"Gated\"\n\
826	         buv_enum_enroll TEST_MODE \"Mode\" on off\n\
827	         buv_gate_enroll TEST_MODE on\n\
828	         buv_port_enroll TEST_PORT \"Port\"",
829	        &[RbtdrfSub::ok_cmd("gated report passes",
830	            "export TEST_MODE=\"off\"\nexport TEST_PORT=\"\"",
831	            "buv_report \"TEST\" \"Gated report\"")],
832	    )
833	}
834	
835	fn rbtdrf_ev_multiscope(dir: &Path) -> rbtdre_Verdict {
836	    let root = match std::env::current_dir() {
837	        Ok(r) => r,
838	        Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("cannot get cwd: {}", e)),
839	    };
840	    let buv = root.join("Tools/buk/buv_validation.sh");
841	
842	    // Two-scope enrollment: ALPHA and BETA
843	    let enrollment = format!(
844	        "set -euo pipefail\nsource '{}'\nzbuv_kindle\nzbuv_reset_enrollment\n\
845	         buv_regime_enroll \"ALPHA\"\nbuv_group_enroll \"Alpha Vars\"\n\
846	         buv_bool_enroll TEST_ALPHA_FLAG \"Alpha flag\"\n\
847	         buv_regime_enroll \"BETA\"\nbuv_group_enroll \"Beta Vars\"\n\
848	         buv_bool_enroll TEST_BETA_FLAG \"Beta flag\"\n\
849	         export TEST_ALPHA_FLAG=\"1\"\nexport TEST_BETA_FLAG=\"bad\"\n",
850	        rbtdrx_native_to_posix(&buv),
851	    );
852	
853	    // Sub 1: vet ALPHA passes (BETA is bad but not in scope)
854	    let script1 = format!("{}buv_vet \"ALPHA\"", enrollment);
855	    match rbtdrf_run_bash(&root, &script1, dir, "sub-0") {
856	        Ok((code, _, _)) if code == 0 => {}
857	        Ok((code, _, _)) => {
858	            return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("alpha scope: expected ok, got exit {}", code));
859	        }
860	        Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("alpha scope: {}", e)),
861	    }
862	
863	    // Sub 2: vet BETA fails
864	    let script2 = format!("{}buv_vet \"BETA\"", enrollment);
865	    match rbtdrf_run_bash(&root, &script2, dir, "sub-1") {
866	        Ok((code, _, _)) if code != 0 => {}
867	        Ok((_, _, _)) => {
868	            return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail("beta scope: expected failure, got exit 0".to_string());
869	        }
870	        Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("beta scope: {}", e)),
871	    }
872	
873	    rbtdre_Verdict::Pass
874	}
875

879	// referenced across rv_rbrr negatives and rs_rbrr_nonempty_prefix smoke.

890	// the bad-dir cases below override to a nonexistent path). Each negative

894	export RBRR_VESSEL_DIR=\"/tmp\"\n\

899	export RBRR_SECRETS_DIR=\"/tmp\"\n\

905	}

927	        "export RBRR_SECRETS_DIR=\"/tmp/nonexistent-rbtdrf-secrets-dir\"")
928	}

930	// Runtime-prefix format rule: ^[a-z][a-z0-9-]*-$ length 2..=11 (rbrr_regime.sh).
931	// Three independent failure modes — one case each.
932	
933	fn rbtdrf_rv_rbrr_bad_runtime_prefix_uppercase(dir: &Path) -> rbtdre_Verdict {

936	}

941	}

949	
950	// Valid synthetic RBRD baseline — every enrolled field passes kindle+enforce
951	// (joint prefix+moniker length 16 <= 30). Recategorized here from the former
952	// rbrr-named block: these exercise RBRD_DEPOT_MONIKER and RBRD_CLOUD_PREFIX,
953	// independent of RBRR after the RBRD-from-RBRR split.

964	
965	fn rbtdrf_rv_rbrd_baseline_valid(dir: &Path) -> rbtdre_Verdict {
966	    rbtdrf_run_probate(dir, RBTDRM_MODULE_RBRD, RBTDRM_PROBATE_RBRD,

977	
978	// Cloud-prefix format rule: ^[a-z][a-z0-9-]*-$ length 2..=11 (rbrd_regime.sh).
979	// Three independent failure modes — one case each.
980	
981	fn rbtdrf_rv_rbrd_bad_cloud_prefix_uppercase(dir: &Path) -> rbtdre_Verdict {
982	    rbtdrf_rv_rbrd_neg(dir, "rbrd-bad-cloud-prefix-uppercase",
983	        &format!("export {}=\"BAD-\"", RBTDRF_VAR_RBRD_CLOUD_PREFIX))

content.content:
1495	## Command Dependency Discipline
1496	
1497	External commands are potential failure points, portability hazards, and supply-chain surface area. Every command invocation outside of bash builtins must earn its place.
1498	
1499	### POSIX Utility Allowlist
1500	
1501	These external commands are accepted in any BUK-based project. Each has no bash 3.2 builtin replacement and is mandated by POSIX on any system that runs bash:
1502	
1503	`chmod`, `cp`, `date`, `find`, `mkdir`, `mktemp`, `mv`, `rm`, `sed`, `sleep`, `sort`, `stty`
1504	
1505	Commands on this list need no justification — they are the irreducible dependency floor.
1506	
1507	### Evicted Utilities
1508	
1509	These commands have bash builtin or declared-dependency replacements. Do not reintroduce them.
1510	
1511	| Evicted | Replacement | Notes |
1512	|---------|-------------|-------|
1513	| `awk` | `read` with IFS, parameter expansion | Field splitting is a builtin |
1514	| `base64` | `openssl enc -base64` | Platform flags differ (GNU `-d` vs BSD `-D`) |
1515	| `cut` | `read` with IFS, parameter expansion | Same as awk — field extraction is a builtin |
1516	| `grep` | `case`, `test`, `[[ =~ ]]` | BCG-blessed pattern matching; see Test vs Bracket Expressions |
1517	| `head` | `read -r` | First-line extraction is a single builtin call |
1518	| `ls` | Glob expansion | `for f in dir/*` iterates without spawning a process |
1519	| `sha256sum`/`shasum` | `openssl dgst -sha256 -r` | Platform names differ; openssl is universal |
1520	| `tr` | `${var//old/new}` parameter expansion | Character replacement is a builtin |
1521	| `wc` | `${#var}`, `${#arr[@]}` | Length measurement is a builtin |
1522	
1523	### Declared Dependency Principle
1524	
1525	Anything beyond bash builtins and the POSIX allowlist is a **declared dependency** — a cost accepted by every consumer of the project. Declared dependencies must appear in the project's consumer-facing documentation (e.g., the dependency inventory in RBS0) with their justification.
1526	
1527	The current declared dependency for Recipe Bottle is `openssl`, which replaces three platform-variant commands (`base64`, `sha256sum`/`shasum`) with a single portable binary present on every target platform.
1528	
1529	### Platform-Variant Command Guidance
1530	
1531	Commands that exist on both GNU and BSD but with incompatible flags are the worst kind of dependency: they work on the developer's machine and fail on the consumer's. The canonical examples:
1532	
1533	- `base64 -d` (GNU) vs `base64 -D` (BSD) — evicted, use `openssl enc -base64 -d`
1534	- `sha256sum` (GNU) vs `shasum -a 256` (BSD) — evicted, use `openssl dgst -sha256 -r`
1535	- `stat -c` (GNU) vs `stat -f` (BSD) — wrap in a BUK function or avoid
1536	
1537	**Rule**: If a command's flags differ between GNU and BSD, either evict it in favor of a portable declared dependency or wrap it in a BUK function that normalizes the interface. Never rely on flag detection or platform sniffing at call sites.
1538	
1539	### LLM Contributor Checkpoint
1540	
1541	LLMs reach for whatever solves the immediate problem without feeling the cumulative dependency cost. A `grep -q` is two characters shorter than a `case` block and the model has seen it a million times — but it adds a process spawn, a portability surface, and an entry in the consumer's implicit dependency graph.
1542	
1543	The POSIX allowlist and eviction table serve as a checkpoint: if a command isn't on either list, stop and justify it before writing the code. The justification must answer: "Why can't a bash builtin or an already-declared dependency do this?"
1544	
1545	---
1546	
1547	## Output and Messaging Patterns
1548	
1549	### Message Hierarchy
1550	
1551	buc_step "Major user-visible operation"     # White, stderr, milestones
1552	buc_log_args "Detail from variables"        # Transcript only, from arguments
1553	command | buc_log_pipe                      # Transcript only, from pipeline
1554	buc_warn "Non-fatal warning"                # Yellow, stderr
1555	buc_die "Fatal error message"               # Red, stderr, exits
1556	
1557	### When to use buc_step vs buc_log_«source»:
1558	
1559	**Use buc_step in:**
1560	- Public command functions (rbv_start, rbv_stop)
1561	- Top-level user-initiated operations
1562	- Major state transitions visible to user
1563	
1564	**Use buc_log_«source» in:**
1565	- Internal helper functions (zrbgo_*)
1566	- Capture functions (*_capture) - keeps stdout clean
1567	- Predicate functions (*_predicate) - no user output
1568	- Forensic/debugging information
1569	- Sub-operations within a larger step
1570	
1571	### ✅ Code Display
1572	
1573	```bash
1574	buc_code "command-to-run --with-args"       # Cyan, for showing commands
1575	```
1576	
1577	### ❌ Anti-Patterns
1578	
1579	```bash
1580	# Never use raw echo for user messages
1581	echo "Starting operation..."
1582	
1583	# Never use comments for runtime information
1584	# Processing items    ❌
1585	buc_log_args "Processing items"  ✅
1586	
1587	# Never mix stdout and stderr purposes
1588	echo "Result: ${z_value}"          # If this is return data
1589	echo "Progress update..." >&2      # If this is progress info
1590	```
1591	
1592	---
1593	
1594	## Integration Patterns
1595	
1596	### Using BCU Utilities
1597	
1598	```bash
1599	# Always prefer BCU functions over raw commands
1600	buc_step    # Instead of echo
1601	buc_die     # Instead of echo + exit
1602	buc_warn    # Instead of echo >&2
1603	```
1604	
1605	---
1606	
1607	## Quick Reference Decision Matrix
1608	
1609	| Situation           | Use This Pattern                                                 |
1610	|---------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------|
1611	| Module structure    | Always split: implementation + CLI                               |
1612	| Need return value   | Check error handling decision tree                               |
1613	| Simple validation   | `test ... \|\| buc_die`                                          |
1614	| Config validation   | Regime archetype: enrollment → enforce → report                  |
1615	| User feedback       | `buc_step` for major milestones, `buc_log_«source»` for forensic trail    |
1616	| Runtime information | `buc_log_«source»` for details, `buc_step` for milestones, never comments |
1617	| Show commands       | `buc_code`                                                       |
1618	| Non-fatal issue     | `buc_warn`                                                       |
1619	| Fatal error         | `buc_die`                                                        |
1620	| Success message     | `buc_success`                                                    |
1621	| Variable expansion  | Always `"${var}"`                                                |
1622	| Control flow after test | `test ... \|\| break/continue/return` (never `&&`)           |
1623	| Conditional tests   | `test` not `[[ ]]`                                               |
1624	| Pattern matching    | `[[ var =~ pattern ]]` only                                      |
1625	| Secret extraction   | `_capture` function, never temp files                            |
1626	| Roll population + return | `_enroll` function (kindle only), `z_«funcname»_«retval»` vars |
1627	| Roll read access    | `_recite` function                                               |
1628	| True/false check    | `_predicate` function                                            |
1629	| Runtime information | `buc_log_«source»`/`buc_step`, never comments                             |
1630	| File paths          | Kindle constants — defined in kindle only                        |
1631	| File reading        | Single-line `$(<file)` with validation                           |
1632	| Function structure  | See Function Patterns tables                                     |
1633	| Error handling      | See Error Handling Decision Tree                                 |
1634	| Forensic detail (vars)  | `buc_log_args "message ${z_var}"` |
1635	| Forensic detail (output)| `command \| buc_log_pipe`         |
1636	
1637	---
1638	
1639	## Module Maturity Checklist
1640	
1641	### Module Structure
1642	- [ ] Implementation file «prefix»_«name».sh exists
1643	- [ ] CLI file «prefix»_cli.sh exists (skip if library/utility module with no direct user commands)
1644	- [ ] Implementation has multiple inclusion detection guard (`Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED`)
1645	- [ ] CLI starts with `set -euo pipefail`
1646	- [ ] Implementation file sources nothing (except within kindle function)
1647	- [ ] CLI header sources only `buc_command.sh`; all other deps sourced in furnish
1648	- [ ] External code accesses module through CLI only (no direct `z*_kindle()` calls from outside)
1649	- [ ] `_cli.sh` files are executable; all other `*.sh` files in the module directory are not
1650	
1651	### Required Functions
1652	- [ ] `z«prefix»_kindle` - first line: `test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die`
1653	- [ ] `z«prefix»_sentinel` - first line: `test "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die`
1654	- [ ] `z«prefix»_furnish` (CLI only) - documents env vars, sources all deps, calls kindle; gates with `buc_doc_env_done`
1655	- [ ] `z«prefix»_enforce` (regime archetype only) - `buv_vet` + custom format checks after kindle
1656	- [ ] All public functions start with sentinel check
1657	- [ ] All internal helpers prefixed with `z«prefix»_`
1658	
1659	### Variable Management
1660	- [ ] All kindle constants (internal `Z«PREFIX»_SCREAMING` and public `«PREFIX»_SCREAMING`) defined exclusively in kindle with `readonly`
1661	- [ ] No kindle constant assignments outside kindle function
1662	- [ ] Mutable kindle state (counters, rolls, builder state) uses lowercase `z_«prefix»_name` — no `readonly`
1663	- [ ] Tinder constants (`«PREFIX»_lower_name`) resolve at source time — pure literals or `${«PREFIX»_lower_name}` expansion of earlier tinder from the same module; no `$(…)` computation; no expansion of kindle constants or runtime variables; placed after `SOURCED` guard
1664	- [ ] All local variables use `z_` prefix
1665	- [ ] All expansions use `"${var}"` pattern (braced, quoted)
1666	- [ ] Parameters use `"${1:-}"` pattern for defensive programming
1667	- [ ] Module state variable `readonly Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1` is the last statement in kindle
1668	- [ ] No bare `$var` or unbraced `"$var"` expansions
1669	- [ ] No tilde in quoted strings — use `${HOME}` instead
1670	- [ ] No `local -i` — use plain local with explicit validation
1671	- [ ] No raw `eval` for value assignment — use `printf -v` after name validation; `${!name}` for reading
1672	
1673	### Error Handling
1674	- [ ] Every command that can fail has `|| buc_die` (`|| buc_warn` only with a human-authored comment granting permission and explaining why non-fatal is safe)
1675	- [ ] `_predicate` functions return 0/1, never die, no output
1676	- [ ] `_capture` functions output once at end or exit 1, no stderr
1677	- [ ] `_enroll` functions set `z_«funcname»_«retval»` return vars, never echo; callers never use `$()` (when applicable)
1678	- [ ] `_enroll` functions called only within kindle (when applicable)
1679	- [ ] `_recite` functions never mutate roll arrays (when applicable)
1680	- [ ] Roll arrays use `z_«prefix»_«name»_roll` naming convention (when applicable)
1681	- [ ] Two-line pattern for capturing: `z_var=$(func_capture) || buc_die`
1682	- [ ] File reads validated: `test -n "${z_content}" || buc_die`
1683	- [ ] Pipelines either: occur inside a `_capture` function, write to temp files with explicit status checks, or explicitly inspect `${PIPESTATUS[@]}`
1684	- [ ] No `test $(command)` — use `grep -q` or `case` for validation
1685	- [ ] Only `_predicate` functions in `if`/`while` conditions — no regular/enroll functions in conditionals
1686	- [ ] No `test ... && break/continue/return` — invert test and use `||`
1687	- [ ] Error blocks use `{ ...; }` not `( ... )` — no `|| (... exit ...)` patterns
1688	- [ ] Isolation subshells (`( ... ) || buc_die`) have `|| buc_die` on every internal command and on the outer boundary
1689	
1690	### Command Substitution Rules
1691	- [ ] NO `local z_var=$(cmd)` — always two-line pattern (`local` swallows exit status)
1692	- [ ] NO pipelines inside `$()` — use `_capture` function or temp files
1693	- [ ] NO unguarded `$()` — every `$(cmd)` must have `|| buc_die` or `|| return 1`
1694	- [ ] NO `$()` on external commands — use temp files; `_capture`/`_recite` for contracted functions only
1695	- [ ] Bash introspection (`compgen`, `declare -F`, `mktemp`) permitted in `$()` — no file-based alternative
1696	- [ ] `$(<file)` is a builtin redirect, not command substitution — needs content validation (`test -n`), NOT a `|| buc_die` guard
1697	- [ ] Temp files for all external command output capture
1698	- [ ] `_capture` and `_recite` functions properly named with suffix
1699	- [ ] `_yawp` functions properly named with suffix
1700	
1701	### Yawp Functions (when applicable)
1702	- [ ] `_yawp` functions use pure bash builtins only — no external commands
1703	- [ ] Return variable `z_«module»_«group»` initialized in kindle as mutable kindle state
