1706	- [ ] Return variable never appears directly in output calls (`buh_line`, `printf`, etc.)
1707	- [ ] No `|| buc_die` at call site (cannot fail)
1708	- [ ] No `buc_die` internally

1710	
1711	### Loop Safety
1712	- [ ] All while-read loops use load-then-iterate pattern
1713	- [ ] All `while read` conditions include trailing-newline guard: `|| test -n "${z_var}"`
1714	- [ ] No file descriptor held open across function calls that may touch stdin
1715

1717	- [ ] No bash 4+ features (associative arrays, `**`, readarray)
1718	- [ ] Use `test` not `[[ ]]` except for pattern matching `=~`
1719	- [ ] Use `=` not `==` in test expressions

1724

1728	- [ ] Internal functions: `z«prefix»_«name»` (snake_case)
1729	- [ ] Internal constants: `Z«PREFIX»_«NAME»` (SCREAMING_SNAKE)
1730	- [ ] Public constants: `«PREFIX»_«NAME»` (SCREAMING_SNAKE)
1731	- [ ] Special functions: `*_predicate`, `*_capture`, `*_enroll`, `*_recite` suffixes
1732	
1733	### Documentation & Visibility
1734	- [ ] Public functions documented with `buc_doc_*` blocks
1735	- [ ] Internal functions documented with `buc_doc_*` blocks (recommended)
1736	- [ ] `buc_doc_shown || return 0` after documentation in functions that have it
1737	- [ ] Runtime info via `buc_log_«source»` (forensic) and `buc_step` (milestones), never comments
1738	- [ ] Details use `buc_log_«source»` (transcript.txt)
1739	- [ ] Comments only for code clarification, not runtime info
1740	- [ ] No commit-message comments (narrating the change, not the code)
1741	- [ ] Major operations use `buc_step` (always visible)
1742	
1743	### Sourcing Restrictions
1744	- [ ] CLI header sources only `buc_command.sh`
1745	- [ ] `z«prefix»_furnish` sources all other dependencies and config files
1746	- [ ] Furnish gates with `buc_doc_env_done || return 0` before any sourcing
1747	- [ ] Credential sourcing documented and never writes to disk
1748	- [ ] No other sourcing anywhere
1749	
1750	### Interface Contamination Discipline
1751	- [ ] Commands accept exactly one canonical form for each argument — no aliases
1752	- [ ] No case folding, prefix stripping, or normalization of inputs
1753	- [ ] No `${1:-default}` for required parameters — missing required args cause `buc_die`
1754	- [ ] `buc_doc_*` output shows the single canonical invocation form
1755	- [ ] Zeroes theory applied: no unexplained state space expansion
1756	
1757	### Code Quality
1758	- [ ] Prefer bash builtins over external tools (parameter expansion vs sed/awk)
1759	- [ ] Use BCU utilities instead of raw bash (buc_die vs echo+exit)
1760	- [ ] Use enrollment for config validation, `buv_dir_exists`/`buv_file_exists` for path checks
1761	- [ ] Consistent error messages (specific and actionable)
1762	- [ ] Proper temp file naming with module prefix
1763	- [ ] No silent failures or ignored conditions
1764	- [ ] No `2>/dev/null` — stderr redirected to temp file, path included in `buc_die` message
1765	- [ ] Temp files never deleted in module code — preserved for forensics
1766	
1767	### Enterprise Safety
1768	- [ ] Crash-fast principle applied throughout
1769	- [ ] No elegant-but-fragile pipeline patterns
1770	- [ ] Secrets never written to disk (use `_capture` functions)
1771	- [ ] Temp files make failures visible and debuggable
1772	- [ ] Every potential error explicitly handled
1773	- [ ] Abstraction layers used (BCU utilities, enrollment infrastructure)
1774	
1775	### Regime Archetype (when applicable)
1776	- [ ] Kindle calls `buv_*_enroll` for each config field
1777	- [ ] Kindle calls `buv_scope_sentinel` after all enrollments, before `KINDLED=1`
1778	- [ ] `z«prefix»_enforce` calls `buv_vet SCOPE` then custom format checks

1781	- [ ] CLI provides `*_render` command using `buv_render`

1783	---
1784	
1785	## Test Execution Patterns
1786

1788	
1789	BCG prohibits subshells for error handling (see **Subshell Exit vs Brace-Group**). Test execution is the principled counterpoint: subshells provide **isolation boundaries** that prevent state leakage between fixtures and cases.
1790	
1791	In bash 3.2, there is no module system, no scope isolation, and no cleanup-on-exit guarantee. When fixture A kindles modules and fixture B kindles the same modules, the kindle guards (`Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED`) from fixture A block fixture B. Subshells solve this: all state — kindle guards, sourced functions, variables — dies at the `)` boundary.
1792	
1793	This is discipline encoded as structure.
1794	
1795	### Three-Layer Model
1796	
1797	Test execution uses three layers separated by two subshell boundaries:
1798	
1799	```
1800	Parent Shell (runner layer)
1801	 ├─ Fixture iteration and status tracking
1802	 └─ Reporting (pass/fail/skip counts)
1803	     │

## Command Dependency Discipline

External commands are potential failure points, portability hazards, and supply-chain surface area. Every command invocation outside of bash builtins must earn its place.

### POSIX Utility Allowlist

These external commands are accepted in any BUK-based project. Each has no bash 3.2 builtin replacement and is mandated by POSIX on any system that runs bash:

`chmod`, `cp`, `date`, `find`, `mkdir`, `mktemp`, `mv`, `rm`, `sed`, `sleep`, `sort`, `stty`

Commands on this list need no justification — they are the irreducible dependency floor.

### Evicted Utilities

These commands have bash builtin or declared-dependency replacements. Do not reintroduce them.

| Evicted | Replacement | Notes |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| `awk` | `read` with IFS, parameter expansion | Field splitting is a builtin |
| `base64` | `openssl enc -base64` | Platform flags differ (GNU `-d` vs BSD `-D`) |
| `cut` | `read` with IFS, parameter expansion | Same as awk — field extraction is a builtin |
| `grep` | `case`, `test`, `[[ =~ ]]` | BCG-blessed pattern matching; see Test vs Bracket Expressions |
| `head` | `read -r` | First-line extraction is a single builtin call |
| `ls` | Glob expansion | `for f in dir/*` iterates without spawning a process |
| `sha256sum`/`shasum` | `openssl dgst -sha256 -r` | Platform names differ; openssl is universal |
| `tr` | `${var//old/new}` parameter expansion | Character replacement is a builtin |
| `wc` | `${#var}`, `${#arr[@]}` | Length measurement is a builtin |

### Declared Dependency Principle

Anything beyond bash builtins and the POSIX allowlist is a **declared dependency** — a cost accepted by every consumer of the project. Declared dependencies must appear in the project's consumer-facing documentation (e.g., the dependency inventory in RBS0) with their justification.

The current declared dependency for Recipe Bottle is `openssl`, which replaces three platform-variant commands (`base64`, `sha256sum`/`shasum`) with a single portable binary present on every target platform.

### Platform-Variant Command Guidance

Commands that exist on both GNU and BSD but with incompatible flags are the worst kind of dependency: they work on the developer's machine and fail on the consumer's. The canonical examples:

- `base64 -d` (GNU) vs `base64 -D` (BSD) — evicted, use `openssl enc -base64 -d`
- `sha256sum` (GNU) vs `shasum -a 256` (BSD) — evicted, use `openssl dgst -sha256 -r`
- `stat -c` (GNU) vs `stat -f` (BSD) — wrap in a BUK function or avoid

**Rule**: If a command's flags differ between GNU and BSD, either evict it in favor of a portable declared dependency or wrap it in a BUK function that normalizes the interface. Never rely on flag detection or platform sniffing at call sites.

### LLM Contributor Checkpoint

LLMs reach for whatever solves the immediate problem without feeling the cumulative dependency cost. A `grep -q` is two characters shorter than a `case` block and the model has seen it a million times — but it adds a process spawn, a portability surface, and an entry in the consumer's implicit dependency graph.

The POSIX allowlist and eviction table serve as a checkpoint: if a command isn't on either list, stop and justify it before writing the code. The justification must answer: "Why can't a bash builtin or an already-declared dependency do this?"

---

## Output and Messaging Patterns

### Message Hierarchy

buc_step "Major user-visible operation"     # White, stderr, milestones
buc_log_args "Detail from variables"        # Transcript only, from arguments
command | buc_log_pipe                      # Transcript only, from pipeline
buc_warn "Non-fatal warning"                # Yellow, stderr
buc_die "Fatal error message"               # Red, stderr, exits

### When to use buc_step vs buc_log_«source»:

**Use buc_step in:**
- Public command functions (rbv_start, rbv_stop)
- Top-level user-initiated operations
- Major state transitions visible to user

**Use buc_log_«source» in:**
- Internal helper functions (zrbgo_*)
- Capture functions (*_capture) - keeps stdout clean
- Predicate functions (*_predicate) - no user output
- Forensic/debugging information
- Sub-operations within a larger step

### ✅ Code Display

```bash
buc_code "command-to-run --with-args"       # Cyan, for showing commands
```

### ❌ Anti-Patterns

```bash
# Never use raw echo for user messages
echo "Starting operation..."

# Never use comments for runtime information
# Processing items    ❌
buc_log_args "Processing items"  ✅

# Never mix stdout and stderr purposes
echo "Result: ${z_value}"          # If this is return data
echo "Progress update..." >&2      # If this is progress info
```

---

## Integration Patterns

### Using BCU Utilities

```bash
# Always prefer BCU functions over raw commands
buc_step    # Instead of echo
buc_die     # Instead of echo + exit
buc_warn    # Instead of echo >&2
```

---

## Quick Reference Decision Matrix

| Situation           | Use This Pattern                                                 |
|---------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Module structure    | Always split: implementation + CLI                               |
| Need return value   | Check error handling decision tree                               |
| Simple validation   | `test ... \|\| buc_die`                                          |
| Config validation   | Regime archetype: enrollment → enforce → report                  |
| User feedback       | `buc_step` for major milestones, `buc_log_«source»` for forensic trail    |
| Runtime information | `buc_log_«source»` for details, `buc_step` for milestones, never comments |
| Show commands       | `buc_code`                                                       |
| Non-fatal issue     | `buc_warn`                                                       |
| Fatal error         | `buc_die`                                                        |
| Success message     | `buc_success`                                                    |
| Variable expansion  | Always `"${var}"`                                                |
| Control flow after test | `test ... \|\| break/continue/return` (never `&&`)           |
| Conditional tests   | `test` not `[[ ]]`                                               |
| Pattern matching    | `[[ var =~ pattern ]]` only                                      |
| Secret extraction   | `_capture` function, never temp files                            |
| Roll population + return | `_enroll` function (kindle only), `z_«funcname»_«retval»` vars |
| Roll read access    | `_recite` function                                               |
| True/false check    | `_predicate` function                                            |
| Runtime information | `buc_log_«source»`/`buc_step`, never comments                             |
| File paths          | Kindle constants — defined in kindle only                        |
| File reading        | Single-line `$(<file)` with validation                           |
| Function structure  | See Function Patterns tables                                     |
| Error handling      | See Error Handling Decision Tree                                 |
| Forensic detail (vars)  | `buc_log_args "message ${z_var}"` |
| Forensic detail (output)| `command \| buc_log_pipe`         |

---

## Module Maturity Checklist

### Module Structure
- [ ] Implementation file «prefix»_«name».sh exists
- [ ] CLI file «prefix»_cli.sh exists (skip if library/utility module with no direct user commands)
- [ ] Implementation has multiple inclusion detection guard (`Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED`)
- [ ] CLI starts with `set -euo pipefail`
- [ ] Implementation file sources nothing (except within kindle function)
- [ ] CLI header sources only `buc_command.sh`; all other deps sourced in furnish
- [ ] External code accesses module through CLI only (no direct `z*_kindle()` calls from outside)
- [ ] `_cli.sh` files are executable; all other `*.sh` files in the module directory are not

### Required Functions
- [ ] `z«prefix»_kindle` - first line: `test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die`
- [ ] `z«prefix»_sentinel` - first line: `test "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die`
- [ ] `z«prefix»_furnish` (CLI only) - documents env vars, sources all deps, calls kindle; gates with `buc_doc_env_done`
- [ ] `z«prefix»_enforce` (regime archetype only) - `buv_vet` + custom format checks after kindle
- [ ] All public functions start with sentinel check
- [ ] All internal helpers prefixed with `z«prefix»_`

### Variable Management
- [ ] All kindle constants (internal `Z«PREFIX»_SCREAMING` and public `«PREFIX»_SCREAMING`) defined exclusively in kindle with `readonly`
- [ ] No kindle constant assignments outside kindle function
- [ ] Mutable kindle state (counters, rolls, builder state) uses lowercase `z_«prefix»_name` — no `readonly`
- [ ] Tinder constants (`«PREFIX»_lower_name`) resolve at source time — pure literals or `${«PREFIX»_lower_name}` expansion of earlier tinder from the same module; no `$(…)` computation; no expansion of kindle constants or runtime variables; placed after `SOURCED` guard
- [ ] All local variables use `z_` prefix
- [ ] All expansions use `"${var}"` pattern (braced, quoted)
- [ ] Parameters use `"${1:-}"` pattern for defensive programming
- [ ] Module state variable `readonly Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1` is the last statement in kindle
- [ ] No bare `$var` or unbraced `"$var"` expansions
- [ ] No tilde in quoted strings — use `${HOME}` instead
- [ ] No `local -i` — use plain local with explicit validation
- [ ] No raw `eval` for value assignment — use `printf -v` after name validation; `${!name}` for reading

### Error Handling
- [ ] Every command that can fail has `|| buc_die` (`|| buc_warn` only with a human-authored comment granting permission and explaining why non-fatal is safe)
- [ ] `_predicate` functions return 0/1, never die, no output
- [ ] `_capture` functions output once at end or exit 1, no stderr
- [ ] `_enroll` functions set `z_«funcname»_«retval»` return vars, never echo; callers never use `$()` (when applicable)
- [ ] `_enroll` functions called only within kindle (when applicable)
- [ ] `_recite` functions never mutate roll arrays (when applicable)
- [ ] Roll arrays use `z_«prefix»_«name»_roll` naming convention (when applicable)
- [ ] Two-line pattern for capturing: `z_var=$(func_capture) || buc_die`
- [ ] File reads validated: `test -n "${z_content}" || buc_die`
- [ ] Pipelines either: occur inside a `_capture` function, write to temp files with explicit status checks, or explicitly inspect `${PIPESTATUS[@]}`
- [ ] No `test $(command)` — use `grep -q` or `case` for validation
- [ ] Only `_predicate` functions in `if`/`while` conditions — no regular/enroll functions in conditionals
- [ ] No `test ... && break/continue/return` — invert test and use `||`
- [ ] Error blocks use `{ ...; }` not `( ... )` — no `|| (... exit ...)` patterns
- [ ] Isolation subshells (`( ... ) || buc_die`) have `|| buc_die` on every internal command and on the outer boundary

### Command Substitution Rules
- [ ] NO `local z_var=$(cmd)` — always two-line pattern (`local` swallows exit status)
- [ ] NO pipelines inside `$()` — use `_capture` function or temp files
- [ ] NO unguarded `$()` — every `$(cmd)` must have `|| buc_die` or `|| return 1`
- [ ] NO `$()` on external commands — use temp files; `_capture`/`_recite` for contracted functions only
- [ ] Bash introspection (`compgen`, `declare -F`, `mktemp`) permitted in `$()` — no file-based alternative
- [ ] `$(<file)` is a builtin redirect, not command substitution — needs content validation (`test -n`), NOT a `|| buc_die` guard
- [ ] Temp files for all external command output capture
- [ ] `_capture` and `_recite` functions properly named with suffix
- [ ] `_yawp` functions properly named with suffix

### Yawp Functions (when applicable)
- [ ] `_yawp` functions use pure bash builtins only — no external commands
- [ ] Return variable `z_«module»_«group»` initialized in kindle as mutable kindle state
- [ ] All `_yawp` functions in a group share one return variable
- [ ] Every yawp call immediately followed by `local -r z_name="${z_«module»_«group»}"` — no exceptions
- [ ] Return variable never appears directly in output calls (`buh_line`, `printf`, etc.)
- [ ] No `|| buc_die` at call site (cannot fail)
- [ ] No `buc_die` internally
- [ ] No stdout or stderr output

### Loop Safety
- [ ] All while-read loops use load-then-iterate pattern
- [ ] All `while read` conditions include trailing-newline guard: `|| test -n "${z_var}"`
- [ ] No file descriptor held open across function calls that may touch stdin

### Bash Compatibility
- [ ] No bash 4+ features (associative arrays, `**`, readarray)
- [ ] Use `test` not `[[ ]]` except for pattern matching `=~`
- [ ] Use `=` not `==` in test expressions
- [ ] No HERE documents (heredocs)
- [ ] Here-strings (`<<<`) single-line piping only; no multi-line
- [ ] Target bash 3.2 minimum
- [ ] Array iteration uses index pattern for `set -u` safety

### Naming Conventions
- [ ] Module prefix: 2-4 lowercase letters + underscore
- [ ] Public functions: `«prefix»_«command»` (snake_case)
- [ ] Internal functions: `z«prefix»_«name»` (snake_case)
- [ ] Internal constants: `Z«PREFIX»_«NAME»` (SCREAMING_SNAKE)
- [ ] Public constants: `«PREFIX»_«NAME»` (SCREAMING_SNAKE)
- [ ] Special functions: `*_predicate`, `*_capture`, `*_enroll`, `*_recite` suffixes

### Documentation & Visibility
- [ ] Public functions documented with `buc_doc_*` blocks
- [ ] Internal functions documented with `buc_doc_*` blocks (recommended)
- [ ] `buc_doc_shown || return 0` after documentation in functions that have it
- [ ] Runtime info via `buc_log_«source»` (forensic) and `buc_step` (milestones), never comments
- [ ] Details use `buc_log_«source»` (transcript.txt)
- [ ] Comments only for code clarification, not runtime info
- [ ] No commit-message comments (narrating the change, not the code)
- [ ] Major operations use `buc_step` (always visible)

### Sourcing Restrictions
- [ ] CLI header sources only `buc_command.sh`
- [ ] `z«prefix»_furnish` sources all other dependencies and config files
- [ ] Furnish gates with `buc_doc_env_done || return 0` before any sourcing
- [ ] Credential sourcing documented and never writes to disk
- [ ] No other sourcing anywhere

### Interface Contamination Discipline
- [ ] Commands accept exactly one canonical form for each argument — no aliases
- [ ] No case folding, prefix stripping, or normalization of inputs
- [ ] No `${1:-default}` for required parameters — missing required args cause `buc_die`
- [ ] `buc_doc_*` output shows the single canonical invocation form
- [ ] Zeroes theory applied: no unexplained state space expansion

### Code Quality
- [ ] Prefer bash builtins over external tools (parameter expansion vs sed/awk)
- [ ] Use BCU utilities instead of raw bash (buc_die vs echo+exit)
- [ ] Use enrollment for config validation, `buv_dir_exists`/`buv_file_exists` for path checks
- [ ] Consistent error messages (specific and actionable)
- [ ] Proper temp file naming with module prefix
- [ ] No silent failures or ignored conditions
- [ ] No `2>/dev/null` — stderr redirected to temp file, path included in `buc_die` message
- [ ] Temp files never deleted in module code — preserved for forensics

### Enterprise Safety
- [ ] Crash-fast principle applied throughout
- [ ] No elegant-but-fragile pipeline patterns
- [ ] Secrets never written to disk (use `_capture` functions)
- [ ] Temp files make failures visible and debuggable
- [ ] Every potential error explicitly handled
- [ ] Abstraction layers used (BCU utilities, enrollment infrastructure)

### Regime Archetype (when applicable)
- [ ] Kindle calls `buv_*_enroll` for each config field
- [ ] Kindle calls `buv_scope_sentinel` after all enrollments, before `KINDLED=1`
- [ ] `z«prefix»_enforce` calls `buv_vet SCOPE` then custom format checks
- [ ] Furnish calls kindle → enforce (ironclad gate)
- [ ] CLI provides `*_validate` command using `buv_report`
- [ ] CLI provides `*_render` command using `buv_render`

---

## Test Execution Patterns

### Principled Subshell Use

BCG prohibits subshells for error handling (see **Subshell Exit vs Brace-Group**). Test execution is the principled counterpoint: subshells provide **isolation boundaries** that prevent state leakage between fixtures and cases.

In bash 3.2, there is no module system, no scope isolation, and no cleanup-on-exit guarantee. When fixture A kindles modules and fixture B kindles the same modules, the kindle guards (`Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED`) from fixture A block fixture B. Subshells solve this: all state — kindle guards, sourced functions, variables — dies at the `)` boundary.

This is discipline encoded as structure.

### Three-Layer Model

Test execution uses three layers separated by two subshell boundaries:

```
Parent Shell (runner layer)
 ├─ Fixture iteration and status tracking
 └─ Reporting (pass/fail/skip counts)
     │
     └─ Fixture Subshell (fixture boundary)
         ├─ _baste — kindle, source, configure
         ├─ Fixture temp dir creation
         └─ Case iteration
             │
             └─ Case Subshell (_tcase boundary)
                 ├─ Per-case BUT_TEMP_DIR and BUTE_BURV_ROOT
                 ├─ Assertions and verification
                 └─ Exit status communicated to fixture
```

&root,
            RBTDGC_HYGIENE_CHECK_VESSEL,
            &[&sigil],
            dir,
            &format!("vessel-{}", sigil),
        ) {
            return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(e);
        }
        count += 1;
    }

    if count == 0 {
        return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!(
            "zero vessels iterated under {} — busted RBRR_VESSEL_DIR resolution",
            vessel_dir
        ));
    }

    rbtdre_Verdict::Pass
}

content.text: The dockerfile-hygiene fixture is the closest model — a static-analysis fixture. Let me read the manifest (registration + suite composition) and the `dh_` case implementations to see the file-walking pattern.

content.content:
1	// Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
2	//
3	// Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
4	// you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
5	// You may obtain a copy of the License at
6	//

9	// Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
10	// distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
11	// WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
12	// See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
13	// limitations under the License.
14	//
15	// Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
16	//
17	// RBTDRM — colophon manifest verification for theurge
18	
19	// Colophon names are projected from the zipper registry into the generated
20	// RBTDGC_* consts (rbtdgc_consts.rs). This module consumes them for the
21	// per-fixture required-colophon manifest and the role→probe mapping. Colophon
22	// existence is now enforced by compilation (this map references the generated
23	// consts directly) plus the build-time diff gate (rbq regenerates and diffs
24	// the consts against the zipper); the former runtime drift check is retired.
25	use crate::rbtdgc_consts::*;
26	
27	// Credential roles are projected from rbcc_Constants.sh into the generated
28	// RBTDGC_ACCOUNT_* consts (rbtdgc_consts.rs) — consumed here and across the access
29	// probe surface. The former hand-written RBTDRM_ROLE_* mirror is retired.
30	
31	/// Map a credential role to its access-probe colophon. Returns None for
32	/// unknown roles. Replaces the former role-as-imprint scheme: each role now
33	/// names its own global tabtarget under the rbw-ac* family.
34	pub fn rbtdrm_credential_check_colophon(role: &str) -> Option<&'static str> {
35	    match role {
36	        RBTDGC_ACCOUNT_GOVERNOR => Some(RBTDGC_CHECK_GOVERNOR),
37	        RBTDGC_ACCOUNT_RETRIEVER => Some(RBTDGC_CHECK_RETRIEVER),
38	        RBTDGC_ACCOUNT_DIRECTOR => Some(RBTDGC_CHECK_DIRECTOR),
39	        RBTDGC_ACCOUNT_PAYOR => Some(RBTDGC_CHECK_PAYOR),
40	        _ => None,
41	    }
42	}
43	
44	// Fixture name consts — single definition per String Boundary Discipline.
45	// Crucible fixtures (charge/quench lifecycle)
46	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_TADMOR: &str = "tadmor";
47	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_MORIAH: &str = "moriah";
48	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_SRJCL: &str = "srjcl";
49	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_PLUML: &str = "pluml";
50	// Bare fixtures (GCP credentials, no container runtime)
51	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_HALLMARK_LIFECYCLE: &str = "hallmark-lifecycle";
52	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_BATCH_VOUCH: &str = "batch-vouch";
53	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_ACCESS_PROBE: &str = "access-probe";
54	// Fast fixtures (no external dependencies)
55	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_ENROLLMENT_VALIDATION: &str = "enrollment-validation";
56	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_REGIME_VALIDATION: &str = "regime-validation";
57	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_REGIME_SMOKE: &str = "regime-smoke";
58	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_HANDBOOK_RENDER: &str = "handbook-render";
59	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_DOCKERFILE_HYGIENE: &str = "dockerfile-hygiene";
60	// Pristine-lifecycle fixture (gate + SA/depot lifecycle cases)
61	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_PRISTINE_LIFECYCLE: &str = "pristine-lifecycle";
62	// Gauntlet canonical-establish fixture (§2: canonical depot levy + governor
63	// mantle + retriever/director invest with per-case precondition probes)
64	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_CANONICAL_ESTABLISH: &str = "canonical-establish";
65	// Skirmish canonical-invest fixture — the no-levy variant of
66	// canonical-establish. Reuses the three investiture cases (governor mantle +
67	// retriever/director invest) against a depot the operator has already levied
68	// by hand; omits depot-levy so the skirmish suite creates no GCP project per
69	// run. Distinction from canonical-establish is precondition, not behavior.
70	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_CANONICAL_INVEST: &str = "canonical-invest";
71	// Gauntlet onboarding-sequence fixture (§3: handbook-walked vessel
72	// construction — inscribe reliquary, enshrine bases, kludge tadmor/ccyolo,
73	// plus one ordain-* case per director-mode handbook track, build-only)
74	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_ONBOARDING_SEQUENCE: &str = "onboarding-sequence";
75	// Self-contained tadmor build fixture (rbw-ts.TestSuite.tadmor): kludges tadmor sentry+bottle
76	// locally and commits each hallmark (so the subsequent tadmor crucible fixture
77	// charges against a clean nameplate). Reuses onboarding's kludge helper minus
78	// its reliquary-stamp witness probe — local kludge has no GCP/reliquary dep.
79	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_KLUDGE_TADMOR: &str = "kludge-tadmor";
80	// Dogfight cloud-build viability fixture — standing-depot sibling to
81	// canonical-invest, proving the cloud-build → summon → run path yields a
82	// runnable artifact with NO crucible charged (the orthogonal axis skirmish
83	// covers). Ordains conjure-mode busybox, summons it, runs a degenerate
84	// container-runtime command proving executability, then abjures.
85	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_DOGFIGHT: &str = "dogfight";
86	// Calibrant fixtures — synthetic deterministic-verdict fixtures driving the
87	// bash blackbox testbench. Internal framework-test plumbing.
88	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_CALIBRANT_VERDICTS: &str = "calibrant-verdicts";
89	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_CALIBRANT_FAIL_FAST: &str = "calibrant-fail-fast";
90	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_CALIBRANT_PROGRESSING: &str = "calibrant-progressing";
91	pub const RBTDRM_FIXTURE_CALIBRANT_SENTINEL: &str = "calibrant-sentinel";
92	
93	// Operation verbs and container roles are generated as RBTDGC_VERB_* and
94	// RBTDGC_CONTAINER_* (rbtdgc_consts.rs) from their canonical bash home in
95	// rbcc_Constants.sh; consumers source those directly. Compound operations like
96	// "kludge sentry" are composed at the call site from RBTDGC_VERB_KLUDGE and the
97	// relevant RBTDGC_CONTAINER_* constant.
98	
99	// Regime-validation contract surfaces — the regime module theurge sources and
100	// the public *_probate entry it calls to drive a staged regime file through
101	// kindle+enforce. Single definition per String Boundary Discipline.
102	pub const RBTDRM_MODULE_RBRR: &str = "rbrr_regime.sh";
103	pub const RBTDRM_PROBATE_RBRR: &str = "rbrr_probate";
104	pub const RBTDRM_MODULE_RBRD: &str = "rbrd_regime.sh";
105	pub const RBTDRM_PROBATE_RBRD: &str = "rbrd_probate";
106	pub const RBTDRM_MODULE_RBRV: &str = "rbrv_regime.sh";
107	pub const RBTDRM_PROBATE_RBRV: &str = "rbrv_probate";
108	pub const RBTDRM_MODULE_RBRN: &str = "rbrn_regime.sh";
109	pub const RBTDRM_PROBATE_RBRN: &str = "rbrn_probate";
110	// Payor/station/oauth/auth regimes — RBK file-based regimes gaining a probate
111	// seam alongside the reference quartet above. RBRP's enforce reaches RBGC's
112	// payor-project regex, so its case supplies an rbgc kindle prereq to the harness.
113	pub const RBTDRM_MODULE_RBRP: &str = "rbrp_regime.sh";
114	pub const RBTDRM_PROBATE_RBRP: &str = "rbrp_probate";
115	pub const RBTDRM_MODULE_RBRS: &str = "rbrs_regime.sh";
116	pub const RBTDRM_PROBATE_RBRS: &str = "rbrs_probate";
117	pub const RBTDRM_MODULE_RBRO: &str = "rbro_regime.sh";
118	pub const RBTDRM_PROBATE_RBRO: &str = "rbro_probate";
119	pub const RBTDRM_MODULE_RBRA: &str = "rbra_regime.sh";
120	pub const RBTDRM_PROBATE_RBRA: &str = "rbra_probate";

172	            RBTDGC_HYGIENE_CHECK_DOCKERFILE,
173	            RBTDGC_HYGIENE_CHECK_VESSEL,

190	            RBTDGC_MANTLE_GOVERNOR,
191	            RBTDGC_INVEST_RETRIEVER,
192	            RBTDGC_INVEST_DIRECTOR,
193	            RBTDGC_CHECK_RETRIEVER,

195	        ]),

197	        // levy — same colophons minus LEVY_DEPOT (depot is operator-provided).
198	        RBTDRM_FIXTURE_CANONICAL_INVEST => Some(&[

content.description: Build rbtdru_cupel.rs / rbtdtu_cupel.rs: two-pass function-aware command-position scanner over all Tools/ bash. Encode BCG Command Dependency Discipline allowlists (POSIX floor, declared deps + openssl, enforced eviction table with replacements). Partition kit-bash (strict) vs GCB-bash (rbgja/rbgjb/rbgjv, looser). Register in rbtdrm_manifest.rs, add to fast suite. First run over veiled bash is discovery — surface violations, then fix in-pace. Then delete ceremony Step 2.

#!/bin/bash
#
# Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
#
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
# You may obtain a copy of the License at
#
#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
#
# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
#
# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
# RBQ Qualify - Qualification orchestrator for tabtarget and colophon health

set -euo pipefail

ZRBQ_SCRIPT_DIR="${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}"

source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buq_qualify.sh"

# Multiple inclusion detection
test -z "${ZRBQ_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "Module rbq multiply sourced - check sourcing hierarchy"
ZRBQ_SOURCED=1

######################################################################
# Internal Functions (zrbq_*)

zrbq_kindle() {
  test -z "${ZRBQ_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module rbq already kindled"

  zbuz_sentinel
  zrbz_sentinel
  zrbcc_sentinel

  test -n "${BURC_TABTARGET_DIR:-}" || buc_die "BURC_TABTARGET_DIR not set"
  test -n "${BURC_TOOLS_DIR:-}"     || buc_die "BURC_TOOLS_DIR not set"

  readonly ZRBQ_TT_DIR="${BURC_TABTARGET_DIR}"
  readonly ZRBQ_PROJECT_ROOT="${BURC_TOOLS_DIR}/.."
  readonly ZRBQ_RBW_DIR="${RBCC_KIT_DIR}"
  readonly ZRBQ_RBW_LAUNCHER="${RBCC_moorings_dir}/${RBCC_launchers_subdir}/launcher.rbw_workbench.sh"

  readonly ZRBQ_KINDLED=1
}

zrbq_sentinel() {
  test "${ZRBQ_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rbq not kindled - call zrbq_kindle first"
}

######################################################################
# External Functions (rbq_*)

rbq_qualify_colophons() {
  zrbq_sentinel

  buc_step "Qualifying RBW colophon registrations"

  local z_fail_files=()
  local z_fail_reasons=()
  local z_checked=0

  local z_file=""
  for z_file in "${ZRBQ_TT_DIR}"/*.sh; do
    test -e "${z_file}" || continue

    local z_basename="${z_file##*/}"

    # Load file lines (load-then-iterate)
    local z_lines=()
    local z_line=""
    while IFS= read -r z_line || test -n "${z_line}"; do
      z_lines+=("${z_line}")
    done < "${z_file}"

    # Only check tabtargets using the RBW workbench launcher
    local z_is_rbw=0
    if (( ${#z_lines[@]} > 1 )); then
      case "${z_lines[1]}" in
        *"${ZRBQ_RBW_LAUNCHER}"*) z_is_rbw=1 ;;
      esac
    fi
    test "${z_is_rbw}" = "1" || continue

    z_checked=$((z_checked + 1))

    # Extract colophon from filename (everything before first delimiter)
    local z_colophon="${z_basename%%.*}"

    # Check colophon exists in z_buz_colophon_roll registry
    local z_found=0
    local z_i=0
    for z_i in "${!z_buz_colophon_roll[@]}"; do
      test "${z_buz_colophon_roll[$z_i]}" = "${z_colophon}" || continue
      z_found=1

      # Verify module file exists
      local z_module="${z_buz_module_roll[$z_i]}"
      test -f "${ZRBQ_RBW_DIR}/${z_module}" || {
        z_fail_files+=("${z_basename}")
        z_fail_reasons+=("module not found: ${z_module}")
      }
      break
    done

    test "${z_found}" = "1" || {
      z_fail_files+=("${z_basename}")
      z_fail_reasons+=("colophon '${z_colophon}' not registered in rbz_zipper")
    }
  done

  buc_log_args "Checked ${z_checked} RBW tabtargets"

  if (( ${#z_fail_files[@]} )); then
    local z_j=0
    for z_j in "${!z_fail_files[@]}"; do
      buc_warn "${z_fail_files[$z_j]}: ${z_fail_reasons[$z_j]}"
    done
    buc_die "Colophon qualification failed: ${#z_fail_files[@]} of ${z_checked} tabtargets"
  fi

  buc_log_args "All ${z_checked} RBW colophons registered"
}

rbq_qualify_context() {
  zrbq_sentinel

  buc_step "Qualifying generated context freshness"

  local -r z_committed="${RBCC_tabtarget_context_file}"
  local -r z_fresh="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbq_context_check.md"

  buz_emit_context "${ZRBQ_TT_DIR}" > "${z_fresh}" || buc_die "Failed to generate context for comparison"

  if ! test -f "${z_committed}"; then
    buc_die "Generated context file missing: ${z_committed} — run tt/rbw-tb.Build.sh"
  fi

  if ! diff -q "${z_fresh}" "${z_committed}" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    buc_die "Generated context file is stale: ${z_committed} — run tt/rbw-tb.Build.sh"
  fi

  buc_log_args "Context file is fresh"
}

rbq_qualify_rust_consts() {
  zrbq_sentinel

  buc_step "Qualifying generated Rust consts freshness"

  local -r z_committed="${RBCC_rbtdgc_consts_file}"
  local -r z_fresh="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbq_rust_consts_check.rs"

  rbz_emit_consts > "${z_fresh}" || buc_die "Failed to generate Rust consts for comparison"

  if ! test -f "${z_committed}"; then
    buc_die "Generated Rust consts file missing: ${z_committed} — run tt/rbw-tb.Build.sh"
  fi

  if ! diff -q "${z_fresh}" "${z_committed}" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    buc_die "Generated Rust consts file is stale: ${z_committed} — run tt/rbw-tb.Build.sh"
  fi

  buc_log_args "Rust consts file is fresh"
}

rbq_qualify_fast() {
  zrbq_sentinel

  buc_step "Running fast qualification"

  buq_tabtargets "${ZRBQ_TT_DIR}" "${ZRBQ_PROJECT_ROOT}" \
    "butctt.*.sh" \
    "buw-SI.*.sh" \
    "z-launcher.sh" \
    # End of exempt list
  rbq_qualify_colophons
  rbq_qualify_context
  rbq_qualify_rust_consts
  rbrn_preflight

  buc_step "Running shellcheck"
  buq_shellcheck "${BURC_TOOLS_DIR}" \
    "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/busc_shellcheckrc" \
    "${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/buq_shellcheck_results.txt"

  buc_step "Fast qualification passed"
}

rbq_qualify_release() {
  zrbq_sentinel

  buc_step "Running release qualification"

  # Phase 1: Fast qualification (tabtargets, colophons, nameplate preflight, shellcheck)
  rbq_qualify_fast

  # Phase 2: Complete test suite (sequential)
  buc_step "Running complete test suite"
  "${ZRBQ_PROJECT_ROOT}/tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.complete.sh"

  buc_step "Release qualification passed"
}

# Pristine/skirmish/tadmor qualification collapsed into rbw-ts suite imprints
# (gauntlet/skirmish/tadmor) — see the suite registry RBTDRC_SUITES in
# rbtdrc_crucible.rs. The tadmor suite preserves the deliberate two-fixture
# (kludge-tadmor, tadmor) sequence; its rationale lives on that registry entry.

# eof

content.text: Now I understand the wiring: `qualify_fast` already shellchecks all of `Tools/`; `qualify_release` runs `qualify_fast` + the `complete` suite. So `cupel` must live in `fast` (dev loop) and `complete` (release gate) — and for parity with `dockerfile-hygiene`, in `service`/`crucible`/`gauntlet`/`skirmish` too. Let me confirm the GCB directories and survey the bash corpus.

content.text: The `rbgj*` family is the Google Cloud Build job scripts — they run inside Cloud Build containers where `gcloud`/`docker`/`jq` live. The docket named 3 dirs; there are **7** (it missed `rbgje`, `rbgji`, `rbgjm`, `rbgjr`). Classic site-enumeration drift. Let me confirm the module-declaration site, then present the Track 2 design with this correction folded in.

input.command: cd /Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster 2>/dev/null; echo "=== lib.rs mod declarations ==="; grep -n "^mod \|^pub mod " Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/lib.rs; echo; echo "=== rbtdre_Disposition + Verdict defs ==="; grep -n "pub enum rbtdre_Disposition\|pub enum rbtdre_Verdict\|Independent\|StateProgressing" Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdre_engine.rs | head -20
input.description: Find module declarations in lib.rs and engine enum definitions

content.subject: Track 2 — BCG command-lint fixture (cupel)
content.description: Build rbtdru_cupel.rs / rbtdtu_cupel.rs: two-pass function-aware command-position scanner over all Tools/ bash. Encode BCG Command Dependency Discipline allowlists (POSIX floor, declared deps + openssl, enforced eviction table with replacements). Partition kit-bash (strict) vs GCB-bash (rbgja/rbgjb/rbgjv, looser). Register in rbtdrm_manifest.rs, add to fast suite. First run over veiled bash is discovery — surface violations, then fix in-pace. Then delete ceremony Step 2.

content.text:
I've surveyed the full surface. Here's the **Track 2 design** for review before I write any code — the docket requires each track be presented first.

## `cupel` — BCG command-lint fixture

**Module layout** (mirrors the `calibrant` precedent — dedicated module, since this is a real analyzer, not a shell-out):
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdru_cupel.rs` — the analyzer + fixture static + cases
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdtu_cupel.rs` — unit tests for the scanner (synthetic bash snippets → expected verdicts). *Worth it here* — unlike the other fast fixtures that just shell out, cupel has real parsing logic that benefits from isolated tests.
- `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_CUPEL = "cupel"` in manifest; added to the `Some(&[])` no-colophon arm
- Declared in `lib.rs`; added to `RBTDRC_FIXTURES`; added to the **same suites `dockerfile-hygiene` rides**: `fast`, `service`, `crucible`, `complete`, `gauntlet`, `skirmish` (this is what gives it both the dev loop via `rbw-ts.fast` and the release gate via `complete`/`rbw-tr`)

**Algorithm — two-pass, function-aware (per locked decision):**
1. **Pass 1 — collect.** Walk all `*.sh` under `Tools/` (incl. `vov_veiled/`). For each, harvest locally-defined function names (`name()` / `function name`). Union across the whole corpus → the local-function set.
2. **Pass 2 — flag.** Re-walk; for each command-position token, pass if it's in {bash builtins, local functions, POSIX floor, declared deps}; **fail-with-replacement** if it hits the enforced eviction table.

**Allowlists encoded from BCG (verbatim from §Command Dependency Discipline):**
- *POSIX floor:* `chmod cp date find mkdir mktemp mv rm sed sleep sort stty`
- *Declared dep:* `openssl`
- *Eviction table (enforced, with BCG's prescribed replacement in the failure message):* `awk→read/IFS`, `base64→openssl enc`, `cut→read/IFS`, `grep→case/[[ =~ ]]`, `head→read -r`, `ls→glob`, `sha256sum/shasum→openssl dgst`, `tr→${//}`, `wc→${#}`
- *Bash builtins:* the standard 3.2 builtin set (hardcoded list)

**Command-position detection** (the hard part — soundness rests on the corpus already being shellcheck-clean, so a lexical scan suffices): tokens at statement start, after `|`, `&&`, `||`, `;`, `$(`, `` ` ``, `if`/`while`/`until`/`!`/`then`/`do`/`{`/`(`. Skip assignments, keywords, comments, `case` labels, here-doc bodies, and quoted strings. No full parser — a command-position lexer.

## One correction to surface (drift, not relitigation)

The locked decision says partition GCB-bash as **`rbgja/ rbgjb/ rbgjv/`**. Reality: there are **7** Google-Cloud-Build job dirs — the docket's list missed `rbgje/ rbgji/ rbgjm/ rbgjr/`. This is the stale-site-enumeration trap. The durable, drift-proof fix: **partition by path prefix `Tools/rbk/rbgj`** (the "Google Job" family) rather than a hardcoded 3-dir list — auto-covers all 7 and any future `rbgjN`. GCB-bash gets a looser allowlist (kit floor + `gcloud docker jq` etc., derived empirically from what those scripts actually invoke and surfaced on first run). I'm reading this as honoring the locked principle ("partition by execution environment"), just fixing the enumeration underneath it.

## Posture

Per the docket, **the first full run over veiled bash is a discovery step, not a pass gate** — I expect real eviction-table hits in `vov_veiled/` bash, and I'll surface them to you before fixing (fixes land in-pace).

Two confirmations before I build:

options.description: Treat any Tools/rbk/rbgj*/ bash as GCB-bash. Drift-proof, honors the 'by execution environment' principle, covers rbgje/rbgji/rbgjm/rbgjr the docket missed.

options.label: Strict 3-dir list
options.description: Encode only rbgja/rbgjb/rbgjv as the docket states. The other 4 GCB dirs get linted as strict kit-bash (likely to flag gcloud/docker/jq).

questions.question: Add a dedicated rbtdtu_cupel.rs unit-test module for the scanner logic?

options.label: Yes, add unit tests
options.description: Test the command-position lexer + allowlist logic against synthetic snippets in isolation. Matches the calibrant precedent; this is the one fast fixture with real parsing logic worth unit-testing.

options.label: No, fixture only

aiTitle: Review Rust coding guidelines before starting

content.text: Good catch — let me read RCG before writing any Rust.

content.content:
1	<!--
2	Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
3	SPDX-License-Identifier: Apache-2.0
4	See LICENSE for terms.
5	-->
6	
7	# Rust Coding Guide (RCG) - Pattern Reference
8	
9	## Core Philosophy
10	
11	RCG is deliberately minimal. Trust Claude's inherent Rust idioms for error handling, struct design, documentation, and general style. This guide covers only project-specific conventions that override or extend standard Rust practice.
12	
13	The primary concern is **minting discipline** — ensuring all identifiers follow the project's prefix naming rules for clarity, grep-ability, and ease of discussion in non-Rust-aware contexts.
14	
15	## Crate Boilerplate
16	
17	Every Rust file that serves as a crate root must include these attributes. This includes `lib.rs`, `main.rs`, and every integration test file in `tests/` (each compiles as a separate crate).
18	
19	```rust
20	#![allow(non_camel_case_types)]
21	#![allow(private_interfaces)]
22	#![deny(warnings)]
23	```
24	
25	- `non_camel_case_types`: Allows prefixed type names like `jjrg_Gallops`
26	- `private_interfaces`: Allows z-prefixed (internal) types in public enum variants
27	- `deny(warnings)`: All warnings are build failures — no warning is acceptable in committed code
28	
29	The `allow` directives must appear after `deny(warnings)` to override it for RCG-specific naming patterns. The `deny` directive ensures warnings never accumulate — a warning today becomes a mystery tomorrow.
30	
31	## Import Discipline
32	
33	`use` blocks churn more than almost anything else in a file — every new symbol referenced adds an import, every removed reference drops one. Format them for **change-proportional diffs**: one item per line, alphabetically ordered.
34	
35	### Rule
36	
37	A braced `use` list with 2+ items places each item on its own line, in alphabetical order, with a trailing comma:
38	
39	```rust
40	// ✅ One per line, sorted — adding/removing a symbol is a single-line diff
41	// at a deterministic position
42	use crate::rbtdrm_manifest::{
43	    RBTDRM_COLOPHON_KLUDGE_BOTTLE,
44	    RBTDRM_COLOPHON_KLUDGE_SENTRY,
45	    RBTDRM_FIXTURE_KLUDGE_TADMOR,
46	};
47	
48	// ❌ Width-packed — adding one symbol reflows the line; two branches each
49	//    adding an import to the same packed line collide on merge
50	use crate::rbtdrm_manifest::{
51	    RBTDRM_COLOPHON_KLUDGE_BOTTLE, RBTDRM_COLOPHON_KLUDGE_SENTRY,
52	};
53	```
54	
55	### Rationale
56	
57	The diff of an import edit should be proportional to the change: one symbol added = one line added, never a reflow of neighbors. One-per-line keeps `git blame` precise (each import traces to the commit that introduced it) and removes the spurious merge conflicts width-packing creates. Alphabetical order makes the insertion point deterministic — there is no "where does this go" decision, and two branches adding different symbols sort to different positions, so they don't collide.
58	
59	### Note on rustfmt
60	
61	This project does not run `cargo fmt`; this discipline is review-enforced. Two of rustfmt's behaviors bear on it:
62	
63	- **Alphabetization** is rustfmt's stable default (`reorder_imports`, on by default) — consistent with this rule.
64	- **One-per-line** (`imports_layout = "Vertical"`) is **nightly-only**. On the stable toolchain, setting it in `rustfmt.toml` is rejected with a warning and silently ignored (measured: `Warning: can't set imports_layout = Vertical, unstable features are only available in nightly channel`). `max_width = 1` would force verticality but is a global sledgehammer — it maximally wraps *all* expressions, not just imports — so it is not a targeted substitute.
65	
66	Net: until a nightly `cargo fmt` is adopted (a larger decision — rustfmt has no imports-only mode and would reformat the whole codebase), one-per-line is held by review, alphabetization happens to match rustfmt's stable default.
67	
68	### Smell Test
69	
70	If a single `use` line carries more than one imported item, or the items are not in sorted order, split and sort.
71	
72	## File Naming
73	
74	### Source Files
75	
76	Pattern: `{cipher}r{classifier}_{name}.rs`
77	
78	| Component | Description |
79	|-----------|-------------|
80	| `{cipher}` | Project cipher (2-5 chars): `jj`, `vv`, `vo`, `cm`, `bu` |
81	| `r` | Rust source marker |
82	| `{classifier}` | Single letter, name typically starts with this letter |
83	| `{name}` | Descriptive snake_case name |
84	
85	Examples:
86	- `jjrg_gallops.rs` — JJ Rust, classifier `g`, "gallops"
87	- `jjrc_core.rs` — JJ Rust, classifier `c`, "core"
88	- `vvcg_guard.rs` — VV Core, classifier `g`, "guard"
89	
90	### Test Files
91	
92	Pattern: `{cipher}t{classifier}_{name}.rs`
93	
94	Tests use `t` instead of `r` after the cipher, with matching classifier:
95	
96	| Source | Test |
97	|--------|------|
98	| `jjrg_gallops.rs` | `jjtg_gallops.rs` |
99	| `jjrc_core.rs` | `jjtc_core.rs` |
100	| `vvcg_guard.rs` | `vvtg_guard.rs` |
101	
102	Module wiring in `lib.rs`:
103	```rust
104	pub mod jjrg_gallops;
105	
106	#[cfg(test)]
107	mod jjtg_gallops;
108	```
109	
110	## Declaration Naming
111	
112	**All public declarations from a source file must start with that file's full prefix.**
113	
114	This is non-negotiable. It enables grep-based discovery and clear provenance in discussions.
115	
116	### Naming Patterns by Type
117	
118	For a file with prefix `jjrg`:
119	
120	| Declaration | Public Pattern | Example | Internal Pattern | Example |
121	|-------------|---------------|---------|-----------------|---------|
122	| Struct | `{prefix}_PascalName` | `jjrg_Gallops` | `z{prefix}_PascalName` | `zjjrg_Output` |
123	| Enum | `{prefix}_PascalName` | `jjrg_PaceState` | `z{prefix}_PascalName` | `zjjrg_ParseMode` |
124	| Function | `{prefix}_snake_name` | `jjrg_load()` | `z{prefix}_snake_name` | `zjjrg_validate()` |
125	| Constant | `{PREFIX}_SCREAMING` | `JJRG_DEFAULT_PATH` | `Z{PREFIX}_SCREAMING` | `ZJJRG_MAX_DEPTH` |
126	| Trait | `{prefix}_TraitName` | `jjrg_Storable` | `z{prefix}_TraitName` | `zjjrg_Parseable` |
127	| Type alias | `{prefix}_AliasName` | `jjrg_Result` | `z{prefix}_AliasName` | `zjjrg_InternalResult` |
128	
129	### Impl Methods
130	
131	Methods on prefixed types also carry the prefix:
132	
133	```rust
134	impl jjrg_Gallops {
135	    pub fn jjrg_load(path: &Path) -> Result<Self, String> { ... }
136	    pub fn jjrg_save(&self, path: &Path) -> Result<(), String> { ... }
137	}
138	```
139	
140	Rationale: When reading `gallops.jjrg_save(path)`, the provenance is immediately clear. In discussions, "call jjrg_save" is unambiguous.
141	
142	## Internal Declarations
143	
144	**The `z`/`Z` prefix applies uniformly to all internal declaration types** — structs, enums, functions, constants, traits, and type aliases. The prefix letter follows the casing context: lowercase `z` for snake_case and PascalCase names, uppercase `Z` for SCREAMING_SNAKE constants. See the Internal Pattern columns in the naming table above.
145	
146	This parallels BCG's `z«prefix»_` convention for bash. The key difference: BCG also uses `z_«name»` for local variables because bash has flat function scope. Rust's block scoping eliminates this need — local variables use normal names without any z-prefix.
147	
148	Internal items use `pub(crate)` visibility to enable testing from separate test files:
149	
150	```rust
151	pub(crate) fn zjjrg_validate_silks(s: &str) -> bool {
152	    // Internal helper, testable from jjtg_gallops.rs
153	}
154	
155	pub(crate) const ZJJRG_MAX_DEPTH: usize = 16;
156	
157	pub(crate) struct zjjrg_Output {
158	    pub(crate) heat_silks: String,
159	}
160	```
161	
162	The `z`/`Z` prefix signals "do not call from outside this module's conceptual boundary" even though Rust visibility permits crate-internal access.
163	
164	### Visibility and Derive Macros
165	
166	When using derive macros like `clap`, internal argument structs (`z{prefix}_*Args`) may appear in public enum variants. Rust warns about this visibility mismatch. The `#![allow(private_interfaces)]` attribute in Crate Boilerplate suppresses this expected noise — the `z` prefix communicates intent to humans even when Rust forces technical exposure.
167	
168	### Struct Field Visibility for Tests
169	
170	When tests construct internal structs directly, both the struct AND its fields need `pub(crate)` visibility:
171	
172	```rust
173	// ❌ Tests can't construct this — fields are private
174	pub(crate) struct zjjrg_Output {
175	    heat_silks: String,
176	    pace_count: Option<u32>,
177	}
178	
179	// ✅ Tests can construct this — fields are accessible
180	pub(crate) struct zjjrg_Output {
181	    pub(crate) heat_silks: String,
182	    pub(crate) pace_count: Option<u32>,
183	}
184	```
185	
186	If tests only READ internal structs (returned by functions), field visibility doesn't matter. But if tests CONSTRUCT them for unit testing serialization, validation, or helper logic, fields must be `pub(crate)`.
187	
188	## Test Functions
189	
190	Test functions in `jjt{classifier}_{name}.rs` files use the test file's prefix:
191	
192	```rust
193	// In jjtg_gallops.rs
194	
195	use super::jjrg_gallops::*;
196	
197	#[test]
198	fn jjtg_load_empty_file() {
199	    // ...
200	}
201	
202	#[test]
203	fn jjtg_save_creates_parent_dirs() {
204	    // ...
205	}
206	```
207	
208	## Visibility Layering
209	
210	| Level | Visibility | Prefix | Testable |
211	|-------|------------|--------|----------|
212	| Public API | `pub` | `{prefix}_` | Yes |
213	| Internal | `pub(crate)` | `z{prefix}_` | Yes |
214	| Implementation detail | (none) | (rare) | No |
215	
216	Prefer `pub(crate)` with `z` prefix over true private for anything that benefits from testing. Reserve true private for trivial implementation details.
217	
218	## Minting Discipline
219	
220	All naming follows the project's minting rules. Key constraints:
221	
222	1. **Terminal exclusivity**: A prefix either IS a name or HAS children, never both
223	2. **Cipher ownership**: All identifiers derive from their project's cipher
224	3. **Grep-ability**: `grep jjrg_` finds all declarations from that module
225	
226	Reference: CLAUDE.md "Prefix Naming Discipline" section.
227	
228	## Interface Contamination Discipline
229	
230	**Interface contamination** is any change that expands the accepted input space, invocation forms, or alternative paths through an interface without explicit specification requirement.
231	
232	### Zeroes Theory
233	
234	Every tolerance, alias, fallback, or alternative path multiplies the enumerated state space of the project. The litmus test for any interface decision: **"How many zeroes got added to the enumerated state space because of this choice?"** If the answer isn't zero, the change requires explicit justification in the specification.
235	
236	### Rules
237	
238	1. **One canonical form**: Every function, parameter, command, and identifier has exactly one accepted form. Do not accept alternatives.
239	2. **No tolerances**: Do not add `strip_prefix`, case folding, alias acceptance, or alternative parse paths unless the specification explicitly requires temporary backwards compatibility.
240	3. **No undocumented defaults**: Do not add `unwrap_or`, `Option` with silent defaults, or fallback values that expand what callers can omit beyond what the interface contract specifies.
241	4. **No silent normalization**: Do not silently transform input to a canonical form. If input doesn't match the canonical form exactly, reject it.
242	5. **Documentation uses canonical form**: All documentation, examples, and generated content must use the single canonical form — never a tolerated alternative, even if the implementation happens to accept it.
243	
244	### Relationship to Other Disciplines
245	
246	- **String Boundary Discipline** is the serialization-specific instance: one const, exact match, no friendly parsing
247	- **Minting Discipline** is the naming-specific instance: one canonical name per terminal prefix
248	- This section is the general principle those specific disciplines instantiate
249	
250	### Smell Test
251	
252	If you can invoke the same operation two different ways (e.g., `"close"` and `"jjx_close"`), the interface is contaminated. If a function silently accepts `None` where the caller should always provide a value, the interface is contaminated. If documentation shows a shorter form than the canonical one, the documentation is contaminated.
253	
254	## String Boundary Discipline
255	
256	**Core principle**: This is an internal system. Strings that cross serialization boundaries (JSON, display, CLI) are produced and consumed by the same codebase. There are no external users typing variants.
257	
258	### Single Definition Rule
259	
260	Any string value that appears in serialization or display must have exactly **one** const definition. Both parse and emit reference that const.
261	
262	```rust
263	// ✅ Single definition - const is the source of truth
264	const JJRG_STATE_ROUGH: &str = "rough";
265	
266	impl jjrg_PaceState {
267	    pub fn jjrg_as_str(&self) -> &'static str {
268	        match self {
269	            Self::Rough => JJRG_STATE_ROUGH,
270	            Self::Bridled => JJRG_STATE_BRIDLED,
271	            // ...
272	        }
273	    }
274	
275	    pub fn jjrg_from_str(s: &str) -> Option<Self> {
276	        match s {
277	            JJRG_STATE_ROUGH => Some(Self::Rough),
278	            JJRG_STATE_BRIDLED => Some(Self::Bridled),
279	            // ...
280	            _ => None,  // Bug upstream, not our problem
281	        }
282	    }
283	}
284	```
285	
286	```rust
287	// ❌ String literal appears twice - immature interface
288	impl jjrg_PaceState {
289	    pub fn jjrg_as_str(&self) -> &'static str {
290	        match self {
291	            Self::Rough => "rough",  // First occurrence
292	        }
293	    }
294	
295	    pub fn jjrg_from_str(s: &str) -> Option<Self> {
296	        match s {
297	            "rough" => Some(Self::Rough),  // Second occurrence - drift risk
298	        }
299	    }
300	}
301	```
302	
303	### No Friendly Parsing
304	
305	Do not accommodate input variants. If the canonical form is `"rough"`, do not also accept `"Rough"`, `"ROUGH"`, or `"r"`. Flexible parsing is for external APIs with human users — not internal systems.
306	
307	```rust
308	// ❌ Web-brain: accommodating sloppy input
309	"A" | "a" | "APPROACH" | "approach" | "Approach" => Ok(Self::Approach),
310	
311	// ✅ Internal system: exact match only
312	JJRN_MARKER_APPROACH => Ok(Self::Approach),
313	```
314	
315	If input doesn't match the canonical const exactly, that's a bug in the producer — not a variant to handle gracefully.
316	
317	This applies equally to error diagnostics. Do not add "did you mean?" suggestions, fuzzy matching, or edit-distance scoring to error paths. The producer and consumer are the same codebase — a mismatched string is a bug to fix, not a typo to guess at. List the valid values and stop.
318	
319	### Serde Integration
320	
321	Prefer `#[serde(rename = "...")]` or `#[serde(rename_all = "lowercase")]` over manual string methods when serde handles both serialization and deserialization. The attribute is the single definition.
322	
323	When manual methods are needed (e.g., for display or CLI), define consts that match the serde representation.
324	
325	### Smell Test
326	
327	If `grep '"rough"'` finds the same string literal in multiple places, the interface is immature. Extract a const.
328	
329	## Constant Discipline (Beyond Strings)
330	
331	String Boundary Discipline covers serialized strings. This section extends to **all** repeated values.
332	
333	### Rule
334	
335	Any magic value that appears 2+ times requires a const. This includes numbers, format strings, and computed values.
336	
337	### Derivation Rule
338	
339	If value B depends on value A, always derive B from A at the use site. Never define B as a separate constant.
340	
341	```rust
342	// ✅ Length derived from the const
343	pub const JJRG_UNKNOWN_COMMIT: &str = "0000000";
344	let short_arg = format!("--short={}", JJRG_UNKNOWN_COMMIT.len());
345	
346	fn zjjrg_is_commit_sha(s: &str) -> bool {
347	    s.len() == JJRG_UNKNOWN_COMMIT.len() && s.chars().all(|c| c.is_ascii_hexdigit())
348	}
349	
350	// ❌ Magic number exists separately — drift risk
351	const COMMIT_LEN: usize = 7;  // Could diverge from JJRG_UNKNOWN_COMMIT
352	format!("--short=7")          // Hardcoded, not derived
353	```
354	
355	### Identity Rule
356	
357	The const name must identify the specific value, not just its category. A category-only suffix is ambiguous and becomes a terminal exclusivity violation the moment a second value appears in the same category.
358	
359	```rust
360	// ✅ Suffix identifies the specific command
361	const JJRGS_CMD_NAME_GET_SPEC: &str = "jjx_get_spec";
362	const JJRLG_CMD_NAME_BIND: &str = "jjx_bind";
363	const JJRLG_CMD_NAME_SEND: &str = "jjx_send";
364	
365	// ❌ Suffix names the role, not the identity — deferred terminal exclusivity debt
366	const JJRGS_CMD_NAME: &str = "jjx_get_spec";
367	const JJRLG_CMD_NAME: &str = "jjx_bind";  // Blocks JJRLG_CMD_NAME_SEND
368	```
369	
370	### Smell Test
371	
372	If `grep '7'` (or any literal) finds the same value used for the same purpose in multiple places, extract a const and derive.
373	
374	## Constructor Discipline
375	
376	When a struct requires non-trivial initialization (timestamps, captured values, computed defaults), define a constructor helper. All creation sites use the helper.
377	
378	### Rationale
379	
380	Scattered struct construction leads to:
381	- Inconsistent initialization (some sites forget fields)
382	- Duplicated capture logic (timestamps, git SHAs)
383	- Drift when struct fields change
384	
385	### Pattern
386	
387	```rust
388	// ✅ Constructor centralizes initialization logic
389	pub fn jjrg_make_tack(
390	    state: jjrg_PaceState,
391	    text: String,
392	    silks: String,
393	    direction: Option<String>,
394	) -> jjrg_Tack {
395	    jjrg_Tack {
396	        ts: timestamp_full(),           // Captured once
397	        state,
398	        text,
399	        silks,
400	        commit: jjrg_capture_commit_sha(),  // Captured once
401	        direction,
402	    }
403	}
404	
405	// All creation sites use the helper
406	let tack = jjrg_make_tack(PaceState::Rough, text, silks, None);
407	```
408	
409	```rust
410	// ❌ Raw struct construction scattered across files
411	// In jjx_slate:
412	jjrg_Tack { ts: timestamp_full(), state, text, silks, commit: capture_sha(), direction }
413	// In jjx_tally:
414	jjrg_Tack { ts: timestamp_full(), state, text, silks, commit: capture_sha(), direction }
415	// In deserializer:
416	jjrg_Tack { ts, state, text, silks: migrated, commit: UNKNOWN.to_string(), direction }
417	```
418	
419	### When to Use
420	
421	- Struct has fields requiring runtime capture (timestamps, git state)
422	- Struct has fields with non-trivial defaults
423	- Struct is created in 2+ locations
424	
425	### Exception
426	
427	Test files may construct structs directly with test values (no runtime capture needed).
428	
429	## Comment Discipline
430	
431	Comments describe what code does permanently, not why it was written this session.
432	
433	### Prohibited
434	
435	- References to pace names, coronets, firemarks (`₢AEAAI`, `₣AB`)
436	- References to heat names or silks
437	- Phrases like "this migration", "for this pace", "current task"
438	- Explanations of what changed (that's what git commits are for)
439	
440	### Permitted
441	
442	- What the code does in general terms
443	- Why a non-obvious approach was chosen (permanent rationale)
444	- Constraints or invariants the code maintains
445	
446	```rust
447	// ✅ Permanent description
448	// Use unknown commit placeholder for legacy data without commit tracking
449	
450	// ❌ Task-focused — meaningless to future readers
451	// Migration: if tack lacks commit, use JJRG_UNKNOWN_COMMIT for ₢AEAAI
452	
453	// ✅ Permanent rationale
454	// Custom deserializer handles legacy format where silks lived on Pace
455	
456	// ❌ Task-focused
457	// Added in tack-struct-rust-migration pace to support old JSON
458	```
459	
460	## Output Discipline
461	
462	**Core rule**: Every crate must route all output through a declared output module. No naked `println!`, `eprintln!`, `print!`, or `eprint!` anywhere except inside that module's implementation.
463	
464	### Why This Exists
465	
466	Raw print statements scatter output decisions across the codebase. This creates problems ranging from cosmetic (inconsistent format) to fatal (MCP servers use stdout for JSON-RPC — a stray `println!` corrupts the transport). A single output module centralizes the decision of where output goes, what format it takes, and how to disable it.
467	
468	### Sentinel Comment
469	
470	Every file that produces output must carry a standardized import with a sentinel comment:
471	
472	```rust
473	// RCG output discipline: all emission via apcrl_*! — no direct println!/eprintln!
474	use apcd::{apcrl_info_now, apcrl_error_now, apcrl_fatal_now};
475	```
476	
477	The tag `RCG output discipline:` is grep-able. The comment restates the rule at the point where violations would occur. Future editors see the prohibition when they open the file, not buried in a spec.
478	
479	### Four Severity Levels
480	
481	Every output module must provide these four levels with consistent semantics:
482	
483	| Level | Semantics | Continues? | Rate |
484	|-------|-----------|------------|------|
485	| `trace` | Development debugging | Yes | High — may compile out in release |
486	| `info` | Operational milestones | Yes | Low — always present |
487	| `error` | Recoverable failure | Yes | Hopefully rare |
488	| `fatal` | Unrecoverable | No — exits process | Terminal |
489	
490	### Line Discipline
491	
492	Every emission is one complete line. The output module appends the newline — callers never include `\n`. Every emission automatically includes the source file and line number of the call site.
493	
494	Output format:
495	```
496	[LEVEL] [file:line] message text
497	```
498	
499	### Naming Convention
500	
501	All macros carry the project's output module prefix. Three suffix families:
502	
503	**Unconditional (`_now`)** — emit and continue (or exit for fatal):
504	
505	```rust
506	xxx_trace_now!("checking entry {}", i);
507	xxx_info_now!("loaded {} entries", count);
508	xxx_error_now!("parse failed: {}", err);
509	xxx_fatal_now!("config missing, cannot start");  // never returns
510	```
511	
512	The `_now` suffix distinguishes unconditional emission from conditional variants and enables tab completion: `xxx_error_` → `now`, `if`, `eq`, `ne`, ...
513	
514	**Boolean conditional (`_if`)** — evaluates condition, emits if true, returns the bool:
515	
516	```rust
517	if xxx_error_if!(items.is_empty(), "no items loaded from {}", path) {
518	    return default_result;
519	}
520	
521	xxx_fatal_if!(config.is_none(), "config file not found");
522	// only reachable if config is Some
523	```
524	
525	`_if` fuses the condition check and the diagnostic. The caller cannot forget to log when the condition triggers.
526	
527	**Comparison conditional (`_eq`, `_ne`, `_lt`, `_gt`, `_le`, `_ge`)** — compares two expressions, emits if the comparison is true, returns the bool. Uses `stringify!()` to capture expression names and `{:?}` to show values:
528	
529	```rust
530	if xxx_error_ne!(items.len(), expected, "dictionary load") {
531	    return fallback;
532	}
533	// emits: [ERROR] [src/apcrd_dictionaries.rs:47] dictionary load: items.len() (0) != expected (1000)
534	
535	xxx_fatal_lt!(available, required, "insufficient memory");
536	// emits: [FATAL] [src/apcap_main.rs:30] insufficient memory: available (64) < required (256)
537	// then exits
538	```
539	
540	The suffix names the error condition — the thing that is wrong. The output shows both expressions and their values automatically.
541	
542	### Variant Matrix
543	
544	Projects implement the variants they need. The full matrix:
545	
546	| | `_now` | `_if` | `_eq` `_ne` `_lt` `_gt` `_le` `_ge` |
547	|---|---|---|---|
548	| **trace** | unconditional | returns bool | returns bool + stringify |
549	| **info** | unconditional | — | — |
550	| **error** | unconditional | returns bool | returns bool + stringify |
551	| **fatal** | emit + exit | exit or fall through | exit or fall through + stringify |
552	
553	`info` has only `_now` — milestones are unconditional by nature.
554	
555	### What RCG Specifies vs. What Projects Own
556	
557	**RCG specifies:**
558	- The discipline (must have a module, must route through it, must carry the sentinel)
559	- The four severity levels and their semantics
560	- The suffix naming convention (`_now`, `_if`, comparison operators)
561	- Line-oriented output with automatic file/line
562	- No naked print statements
563	
564	**Projects specify:**
565	- The prefix (`apcrl_`, `vvco_`, etc.)
566	- The output target (stdout, stderr, buffer, file)
567	- Trace gating strategy (`cfg!(debug_assertions)`, feature flag, runtime toggle)
568	- Whether the module is macro-based or object-based
569	- Which comparison variants to implement
570	
571	### Relationship to Other Disciplines
572	
573	Output Discipline is an instance of Interface Contamination Discipline applied to program output. Just as there must be one canonical form for each input, there must be one canonical path for each output. A stray `println!` is output contamination — an undocumented emission path that bypasses format, level, and routing decisions.
574	
575	## File Size Discipline
576	
577	Large files degrade code quality by hiding patterns and encouraging copy-paste.
578	
579	### Thresholds
580	
581	| Lines | Action |
582	|-------|--------|
583	| <500 | Good |
584	| 500-800 | Consider splitting by responsibility |
585	| >800 | **Stop and ask** before continuing |
586	
587	### When >800 Lines
588	
589	Do not autonomously refactor. Instead:
590	1. Note the file size in your response
591	2. Identify natural split points (types, serde, validation, operations)
592	3. Ask the user how to proceed
593	
594	### Natural Split Boundaries
595	
596	Large files typically split into 2-3 smaller files by responsibility. Common boundaries:
597	- Data definitions vs. operations on that data
598	- Core logic vs. serialization concerns
599	- Public API vs. internal helpers
600	
601	All split files keep the same cipher prefix for grep-ability.
602	
603	## Specification Authority Discipline
604	
605	**If the specification doesn't call for it, don't build it.**
606	
607	When implementing a module, the specification is the complete description of required behavior. Do not invent features, diagnostics, or affordances beyond what the spec describes — even when they seem "obviously helpful." If you believe a capability is missing from the spec, **stop and ask** rather than silently adding it.
608	
609	### Why This Exists
610	
611	LLM agents have strong instincts toward user-friendliness inherited from web development: fuzzy matching, "did you mean?" suggestions, graceful degradation, helpful error recovery. In an internal system with a perfect protocol, these instincts produce non-load-bearing complexity. The cost isn't just the code — it's the expanded state space (Zeroes Theory) and the false signal that the system tolerates imprecision.
612	
613	### Rules
614	
615	1. **Spec is the authority**: Implement what the spec says. Not more, not less.
616	2. **No speculative features**: Do not add capabilities "because they'll be useful." If they're useful, they'll be specified.
617	3. **Stop and ask on invention**: If you're about to write a non-trivial algorithm (edit distance, scoring heuristics, search strategies, caching layers) that the spec doesn't mention, that's a gate — ask the user whether it belongs.
618	4. **Error paths are not product features**: Error messages should identify the failure and list valid options. They should not attempt to guess the user's intent.
619	
620	### Smell Test
621	
622	If you're writing code that makes the system "nicer" rather than "more correct," stop. Niceness that isn't specified is complexity that isn't earned.
623	
624	## Load-Bearing Complexity
625	
626	Every element in a system must carry weight — its removal would create a gap between intent and behavior. When similar things differ, the difference must be load-bearing or the code should be homogenized.
627	
628	Rust's type system enforces much of this mechanically: unused fields warn, unused variants warn, dead code warns. The disciplines above — Interface Contamination (no unearned input forms), Specification Authority (no unearned features), Constant Discipline (no unearned literals), Constructor Discipline (no unearned construction sites) — are each instantiations of this principle applied to specific domains. When evaluating a new pattern or extraction, ask: "Is this element load-bearing?" If not, it doesn't belong.
629	
630	## What RCG Does Not Cover
631	
632	Trust Claude's Rust idioms for:
633	
634	- Error handling (`Result<T, E>` patterns)
635	- Documentation (`///` doc comments)
636	- Module organization beyond naming
637	- Struct field design
638	- Lifetime annotations
639	- Generic patterns
640	- Async patterns
641	- Macro design
642

707	## Test File Extraction Checklist
708	
709	When extracting inline tests from `{cipher}r{x}_{name}.rs` to `{cipher}t{x}_{name}.rs`:
710	
711	### Source File Changes
712	- [ ] Remove entire `#[cfg(test)] mod tests { ... }` block
713	- [ ] Change internal functions from `fn` to `pub(crate) fn` if tests call them
714	- [ ] Change internal structs from `struct` to `pub(crate) struct` if tests construct them
715	- [ ] Add `pub(crate)` to struct fields if tests construct the struct
716	
717	### New Test File
718	- [ ] File named `{cipher}t{x}_{name}.rs` (matching source classifier)
719	- [ ] Copyright header copied from source
720	- [ ] Import: `use super::{cipher}r{x}_{name}::*;`
721	- [ ] Additional imports for types from sibling modules as needed
722	- [ ] Test functions renamed from `test_*` to `{cipher}t{x}_*`
723	- [ ] Each test function has `#[test]` attribute
724	- [ ] NO `#[cfg(test)]` wrapper — lib.rs handles that
725	- [ ] NO `mod tests` wrapper — functions go directly in file
726	- [ ] Helper functions (e.g., `create_test_data()`) can remain unprefixed
727	
728	### lib.rs Wiring
729	- [ ] Add `#[cfg(test)] mod {cipher}t{x}_{name};` declaration
730	- [ ] Place after corresponding `pub mod {cipher}r{x}_{name};`
731	
732	### Verification
733	- [ ] `cargo build` succeeds
734	- [ ] `cargo test` runs all extracted tests
735	- [ ] Test count matches pre-extraction count
736	
737	## Module Maturity Checklist
738	
739	### File Structure
740	- [ ] Source file: `{cipher}r{x}_{name}.rs`
741	- [ ] Test file: `{cipher}t{x}_{name}.rs` (if tests exist)
742	- [ ] Every crate root has `#![deny(warnings)]`
743	- [ ] Every crate root has `#![allow(non_camel_case_types)]` (after deny)
744	- [ ] Every crate root has `#![allow(private_interfaces)]` (after deny)
745	- [ ] `lib.rs` declares source module: `pub mod {cipher}r{x}_{name};`
746	- [ ] `lib.rs` declares test module: `#[cfg(test)] mod {cipher}t{x}_{name};`
747	
748	### Naming Conventions
749	- [ ] All public structs/enums: `{prefix}_PascalName`
750	- [ ] All public functions: `{prefix}_snake_name`
751	- [ ] All public constants: `{PREFIX}_SCREAMING`
752	- [ ] All impl methods: `{prefix}_method_name`
753	- [ ] All internal structs/enums: `z{prefix}_PascalName` with `pub(crate)`
754	- [ ] All internal functions: `z{prefix}_snake_name` with `pub(crate)`
755	- [ ] All internal constants: `Z{PREFIX}_SCREAMING` with `pub(crate)`
756	- [ ] All internal traits/aliases: `z{prefix}_Name` with `pub(crate)`
757	- [ ] All test functions: `{cipher}t{x}_test_name`
758	- [ ] Local variables: normal names (no z-prefix — Rust block scoping suffices)
759	
760	### Visibility Discipline
761	- [ ] Public API uses `pub`
762	- [ ] All internal declarations use `pub(crate)` with `z`/`Z` prefix (every type, not just functions)
763	- [ ] Structs constructed by tests have `pub(crate)` fields
764	- [ ] True private reserved for trivial implementation details
765	
766	### Test Organization
767	- [ ] Tests in separate `{cipher}t{x}_*.rs` files, not inline
768	- [ ] Test file imports source module with `use super::*`
769	- [ ] Test functions prefixed with test file's prefix
770	- [ ] Helper functions local to test file (no prefix needed)
771	
772	### Specification Authority Discipline
773	- [ ] Every function and algorithm traces to a spec requirement
774	- [ ] No "helpful" features invented beyond what the spec describes
775	- [ ] No non-trivial algorithms (edit distance, scoring, fuzzy matching) without spec basis
776	- [ ] Error paths list valid options — they do not guess intent
777	
778	### Interface Contamination Discipline
779	- [ ] Functions accept exactly one canonical form for each parameter — no aliases
780	- [ ] No `strip_prefix`, case folding, or input normalization that expands accepted forms
781	- [ ] No silent defaults that expand what callers can omit beyond the interface contract
782	- [ ] Documentation and examples use the canonical form exclusively
783	- [ ] Zeroes theory applied: no unexplained state space expansion
784	
785	### String Boundary Discipline
786	- [ ] Each serialized/displayed string value has exactly one `const` definition
787	- [ ] `from_str` and `as_str` methods both reference the same const
788	- [ ] No "friendly" parsing variants (no `"A" | "a" | "APPROACH"`)
789	- [ ] `grep` for any string literal finds at most one definition (plus tests)
790	- [ ] Serde attributes used where possible (`rename`, `rename_all`)
791	- [ ] Manual string methods only when serde insufficient (CLI, display)
792	
793	### Constant Discipline
794	- [ ] Magic values appearing 2+ times extracted to const
795	- [ ] Derived values (e.g., length) computed from source const, not hardcoded
796	- [ ] No duplicate literals for same semantic value
797	- [ ] Const names identify the specific value, not just the category (Identity Rule)
798	
799	### Constructor Discipline
800	- [ ] Structs with runtime captures (timestamps, git SHA) have constructor helpers
801	- [ ] All creation sites use the constructor, not raw struct syntax
802	- [ ] Constructor named `{prefix}_make_{type}` or `{prefix}_new`
803	- [ ] Test files exempt (may construct with literal test values)
804	
805	### Comment Discipline
806	- [ ] No references to pace/heat names, coronets, firemarks
807	- [ ] No task-focused language ("this migration", "for this pace")
808	- [ ] Comments describe permanent purpose, not current task
809	
810	### Output Discipline
811	- [ ] Crate has a declared output module (e.g., `{cipher}rl_log.rs`)
812	- [ ] No naked `println!`, `eprintln!`, `print!`, or `eprint!` outside the output module
813	- [ ] Files producing output carry the sentinel comment: `// RCG output discipline: ...`
814	- [ ] Four severity levels implemented: trace, info, error, fatal
815	- [ ] All emissions are line-oriented with automatic file/line
816	- [ ] `_now` suffix on unconditional variants
817	- [ ] `_if` suffix on boolean conditional variants (returns bool)
818	- [ ] Comparison variants (`_eq`, `_ne`, `_lt`, `_gt`, `_le`, `_ge`) stringify arguments
819	- [ ] `fatal` variants exit the process (never return)
820	- [ ] `grep -rn 'println!\|eprintln!\|print!\|eprint!' src/` finds only the output module
821	
822	### File Size Discipline
823	- [ ] File under 500 lines (good)
824	- [ ] File 500-800 lines (noted, consider future split)
825	- [ ] File over 800 lines (**stop and ask** before continuing)
826

<!--
